description: Failing to clearly identify who publishes the content, who the authors are, and how to contact the publisher. Sites that are opaque about authorship and ownership tend to be evaluated less favourably, as trust signals are a factor alongside content quality.
source_title: Creating Helpful People First Content

title: Blocking Googlebot Access to AMP Pages, Canonical Pages, or Structured Data Links
description: Using robots.txt rules that prevent Googlebot from crawling the canonical page, AMP page, or links within structured data will cause AMP pages to not appear in Google Search, undermining AMP indexing and eligibility for AMP-related features.
source_title: AMP on Google Search – Guidelines, How It Works, Enhancing, Validating, and Removing

title: Omitting Required Properties on Course Detail Pages
description: Course detail pages that are missing the required 'description' or 'name' properties in their Course structured data will not be eligible for the course list rich result feature.
source_title: Course List Structured Data – Course Discoverability on Google

title: Implementing Course Structured Data Without the Host Carousel ItemList Pattern
description: Placing full Course structured data only on a single page without a summary/list page using ItemList structured data prevents the host carousel from being triggered, reducing discoverability of multiple courses.
source_title: Course List Structured Data – Course Discoverability on Google

title: Creating Content Primarily for Search Engines Rather Than People
description: Producing content whose primary purpose is to rank in search rather than genuinely help users. This includes targeting specific search queries without truly answering the underlying question, summarising what others have said without adding original value, or writing to a specific word count based on beliefs about what Google rewards. Such content tends to perform poorly as Google's systems improve at identifying it.
source_title: Creating Helpful People First Content

title: Omitting Subscription or Paywall Structured Data When Article Structured Data Is Present
description: When content is behind a paywall or requires user registration, Article structured data alone is insufficient. Failing to also add structured data for subscription and paywalled content alongside Article markup can result in Google misrepresenting access requirements or treating the content as cloaked.
source_title: Article Structured Data – News, Blog, and Sports Articles

title: Using the Wrong Schema.org Article Type for the Content
description: Article structured data must use one of three supported schema.org types: Article, NewsArticle, or BlogPosting. Using an unsupported or incorrect type (e.g., using BlogPosting for a news article or a non-Article type entirely) reduces Google's ability to correctly interpret and classify the content.
source_title: Article Structured Data – News, Blog, and Sports Articles

title: Using URL Inspection Tool for Large-Scale Re-indexing Requests
description: The URL Inspection tool is designed for individual URL requests and has a quota limit. Using it to request re-indexing of large numbers of URLs is the wrong approach; a sitemap should be submitted instead.
source_title: Ask Google to Recrawl Your URLs

title: Expecting Guaranteed Immediate Crawling or Indexing After Recrawl Request
description: Submitting a recrawl request does not guarantee immediate crawling or indexing. Google processes requests based on available resources and crawling algorithms, so assuming instant results is a strategic mistake.
source_title: Ask Google to Recrawl Your URLs

title: Blocking a Page with robots.txt While Expecting noindex to Work
description: If a page is blocked by robots.txt, crawlers cannot access it and will never see the noindex directive. As a result, the page may still appear in search results if other pages link to it, making the noindex rule completely ineffective.
source_title: Block Search Indexing with noindex

title: Specifying noindex Directive Inside robots.txt
description: Google does not support the noindex directive when placed inside a robots.txt file. The noindex rule must appear either in the HTML head section as a meta tag or in an HTTP response header (X-Robots-Tag). Using robots.txt to express noindex will be ignored by Google.
source_title: Block Search Indexing with noindex

title: Using QAPage Markup on Flashcard Pages
description: Flashcard pages must use the Quiz schema.org type, not QAPage markup. QAPage is reserved for single Q&A pages containing one question followed by user-submitted answers. Applying QAPage markup to flashcard pages is an incorrect implementation.
source_title: Education Q&A Structured Data – Flashcard Pages

title: Using noindex Meta Tag for Non-HTML Resources
description: For non-HTML resources such as PDFs, video files, and image files, the noindex meta tag cannot be used. The correct method is to use the X-Robots-Tag HTTP response header. Attempting to embed a meta tag in non-HTML files will not be recognized by crawlers.
source_title: Block Search Indexing with noindex

title: Serving Completely Different Content to Crawlers vs. Users via Dynamic Rendering
description: Dynamic rendering is only acceptable when the rendered content served to bots is substantially similar to what users see. Serving meaningfully different content to crawlers through a dynamic rendering setup constitutes cloaking, which violates Google's guidelines.
source_title: Dynamic Rendering as a Workaround for JavaScript Content

title: Hiring an SEO for Simple, Small Sites When Free Resources Suffice
description: For small sites with straightforward content, paying for SEO services is often unnecessary. Google's free resources — including documentation, Search Console, and the SEO Starter Guide — are typically sufficient without professional SEO engagement.
source_title: Do You Need an SEO

title: Using Dynamic Rendering as a Permanent Solution Instead of Server-Side or Static Rendering
description: Dynamic rendering is explicitly a workaround, not a recommended long-term solution. Relying on it permanently instead of migrating to server-side rendering, static rendering, or hydration introduces unnecessary complexity, resource overhead, and ongoing maintenance burden.
source_title: Dynamic Rendering as a Workaround for JavaScript Content

title: Using an Inappropriate or Policy-Violating Favicon
description: Google will not show favicons deemed inappropriate, including those containing pornography or hate symbols. Such favicons are replaced with a default icon, negating any branding benefit in search results.
source_title: Favicons in Google Search – Guidelines and Requirements

title: Frequently Changing the Favicon URL
description: The favicon URL must be stable and should not be changed frequently. Frequent URL changes can delay or prevent the favicon from appearing consistently in Google Search results.
source_title: Favicons in Google Search – Guidelines and Requirements

title: Omitting subscription/paywall structured data alongside Article markup
description: When content requires subscription or user registration, structured data for subscription and paywalled content must be added alongside Article structured data. Omitting it creates an inconsistency between what users see and what Google indexes, risking a cloaking violation.
source_title: Article Structured Data – News, Blog, and Sports Articles

title: Using incorrect schema.org type for Article structured data
description: Article objects must be based on Article, NewsArticle, or BlogPosting. Using other schema.org types (e.g., WebPage, CreativeWork) will not be recognised correctly. Additionally, using NewsArticle for blog posts or BlogPosting for news content is semantically incorrect and reduces the quality of signals sent to Google.
source_title: Article Structured Data – News, Blog, and Sports Articles

title: Using Organisation type for individual human authors in author markup
description: Author markup should use a Person type for individual authors and an Organisation type only for organisations. Misidentifying a human author as an Organisation reduces clarity about authorship and undermines Google's ability to correctly attribute content.
source_title: Article Structured Data – News, Blog, and Sports Articles

title: Relying on site: operator as a definitive index audit tool
description: Using the site: operator to get a complete or authoritative list of indexed URLs is unreliable. It does not return all indexed URLs under a given prefix and does not rank results in any meaningful order. The URL Inspection tool in Search Console is the recommended approach for reliable debugging.
source_title: Debugging with Google Search Operators

title: Using search operators instead of URL Inspection tool for reliable debugging
description: Search operators are subject to indexing and retrieval limits, making them less reliable than the URL Inspection tool in Search Console for debugging. Depending solely on search operators for debugging decisions risks acting on incomplete or inaccurate data.
source_title: Debugging with Google Search Operators

title: Blocking Resources Required for Rendering from Googlebot
description: If JavaScript files, CSS, images, or other resources necessary for rendering a page are blocked from Googlebot (e.g., via robots.txt), the Web Rendering Service cannot fully render the page, potentially causing content to be missed during indexing.
source_title: Developer's Guide to Search

title: Relying Solely on Client-Side Rendering for Important Content
description: Important content loaded exclusively via client-side JavaScript may not be indexed reliably. Asynchronous content loading introduces delays and uncertainty between crawling and indexing. Server-side rendering or static site generation should be used where possible to ensure content is present in the initial HTML response.
source_title: Developer's Guide to Search

title: Failing to share product data with Google through structured data or Merchant Center feeds
description: Ecommerce sites that do not share product data with Google via structured data on site pages or Google Merchant Center feeds make it harder for Google to find, parse, and display their content across Google Search and other surfaces. This directly limits discoverability and shopping journey coverage.
source_title: Ecommerce SEO – Overview and Guide Structure

title: Mobile Version Containing Less Content or Different Metadata Than Desktop
description: Because Google primarily crawls and indexes the mobile version of pages, a mobile version that omits content, uses different meta tags, or contains different structured data compared to the desktop version will result in that content not being indexed.
source_title: Developer's Guide to Search

title: Not Including Large, High-Quality Images in Discover Content
description: Failing to include compelling, high-quality images of at least 1200 pixels wide — or not enabling the max-image-preview:large setting — reduces the likelihood of content appearing in Google Discover.
source_title: Google Discover – How Content Is Selected and Best Practices

title: Exceeding 5000 Characters in Textual Properties of Dataset Structured Data
description: Google Dataset Search only uses the first 5000 characters of any textual property. Providing textual content beyond this limit wastes markup and gives a false impression that the full content will be indexed and used for discovery.
source_title: Dataset Structured Data – Scientific and Research Data Discovery

title: Using breadcrumb markup techniques outside Google's structured data guidelines
description: Implementing breadcrumb structured data using techniques that violate Google's general structured data guidelines, such as cloaking structured data or marking up content not visible to users. This can result in a manual action against the site.
source_title: Breadcrumb Structured Data – BreadcrumbList for Site Navigation

title: Creating LibrarySystem Entities Without at Least One Library Member Entity
description: Every LibrarySystem entity requires at least one Library member entity. Omitting Library member entities from a LibrarySystem will result in an invalid feed structure that fails compliance requirements for the BorrowAction implementation.
source_title: Book Actions Structured Data – Enabling Buy and Borrow Actions

title: Using ISBN-10 Instead of ISBN-13 for Book Feed Reconciliation
description: Google Search uses ISBN as the main reconciliation signal to match feed data to its own knowledge graph. Google prefers ISBN-13, so using ISBN-10 or omitting ISBNs reduces match accuracy and may prevent book actions from appearing in search results.
source_title: Book Actions Structured Data – Enabling Buy and Borrow Actions

title: Implementing Book Actions Without Partnering With Google
description: Participation in the Book Actions feature is limited to approved partners. Attempting to implement book actions structured data without submitting an interest form and receiving approval from Google will not result in the feature being surfaced in search results.
source_title: Book Actions Structured Data – Enabling Buy and Borrow Actions

title: Hosting the Book Actions Feed at an Insecure Location
description: The feed file must be hosted at a secure location. Hosting it over an insecure connection (e.g., plain HTTP) violates implementation requirements and may prevent Google from successfully fetching the feed.
source_title: Book Actions Structured Data – Enabling Buy and Borrow Actions

title: Using Dataset Structured Data to Describe Data Values Instead of Metadata
description: Dataset structured data should describe information about the dataset itself (metadata), not the values contained within it. Attempting to encode actual dataset values or content rather than descriptive metadata (name, description, creator, distribution) misuses the schema and defeats its discovery purpose.
source_title: Dataset Structured Data – Scientific and Research Data Discovery

title: Using Non-Recommended Formats Instead of JSON-LD for Dataset Structured Data
description: Google recommends schema.org JSON-LD as the preferred format for dataset structured data. Implementing dataset markup in other formats (e.g., Microdata or RDFa) goes against Google's explicit recommendation and may result in suboptimal parsing or discovery outcomes.
source_title: Dataset Structured Data – Scientific and Research Data Discovery

title: Including non-canonical or duplicate URLs in a sitemap
description: When the same content is accessible through multiple URLs, only the canonical URL should be included in the sitemap. Including non-canonical URLs wastes crawl budget and may send conflicting signals about which version of a page should be indexed.
source_title: Build and Submit a Sitemap to Google

title: Hosting a sitemap in a subdirectory without Search Console submission and expecting it to affect the full site
description: Unless a sitemap is submitted through Search Console, it only affects URLs that are descendants of the directory where it is hosted. A sitemap placed in a subdirectory (e.g., /blog/sitemap.xml) without Search Console submission will not cover URLs outside that directory, leaving much of the site uncovered.
source_title: Build and Submit a Sitemap to Google

title: Expecting Immediate Reflection of Business Information Changes in Search Results
description: Assuming that changes made to business details, structured markup, or Business Profile will appear in search results immediately. Google's systems require approximately one week to find and update site details after changes are made, so expecting instant updates leads to premature troubleshooting or duplicate actions.
source_title: Business Details in Google Search and Maps

title: Not Verifying Website Ownership in Search Console Before Managing Business Presence
description: Attempting to manage how business information appears in Google Search without first verifying website ownership in Search Console. Verification is the required first step to establish an official web presence and gain the ability to monitor and understand how Google displays site information.
source_title: Business Details in Google Search and Maps

title: Reporting Knowledge Panel Errors Before Sufficient Crawl Time Has Passed
description: Submitting feedback about incorrect knowledge panel information before at least one week has passed since the marked-up page was last crawled by Google. The feedback mechanism is only appropriate to use after this minimum time window, meaning premature reports may be based on stale data that hasn't yet been processed.
source_title: Business Details in Google Search and Maps

title: Exceeding sitemap size limits without splitting into multiple sitemaps
description: A single sitemap is limited to 50MB uncompressed or 50,000 URLs. Exceeding either limit without breaking the sitemap into multiple files and using a sitemap index will result in an invalid sitemap that Google may not fully process.
source_title: Build and Submit a Sitemap to Google

title: Artificially Updating Dates Without Substantial Content Changes
description: Modifying a page's date to a newer value solely to make content appear fresh — without making meaningful updates to the content — is a misleading practice flagged against byline date guidelines.
source_title: Byline Dates – Adding Publication Dates to Search Results

title: Mismatch Between Visible Date and Structured Data Date
description: When the user-visible date on a page does not match the date specified in structured data (datePublished/dateModified), it creates inconsistency that reduces the reliability of date signals for Google's algorithms.
source_title: Byline Dates – Adding Publication Dates to Search Results

title: Omitting a User-Visible Date From the Page
description: Relying solely on structured data without also displaying a prominently labelled date to users reduces the signals available for Google to determine accurate byline dates.
source_title: Byline Dates – Adding Publication Dates to Search Results

title: Using Non-ISO 8601 Date Formats in Structured Data
description: Providing dates in structured data (datePublished/dateModified) in formats other than ISO 8601 does not comply with Google's requirements and may prevent accurate date parsing.
source_title: Byline Dates – Adding Publication Dates to Search Results

title: Attributing traffic drops to site-specific issues without checking broader web trends
description: Changes in user behaviour due to new trends or seasonality can reduce demand for certain queries across the entire web, not just for a single site. Failing to use Google Trends to verify whether the drop is site-specific or industry-wide leads to misdiagnosis and wasted remediation efforts.
source_title: Debug Google Search Traffic Drops

title: Failing to check Search Console Data Anomalies before investigating traffic drops
description: The first step when investigating a traffic drop should be checking the Search Console Data Anomalies page. Skipping this step may lead to misattributing a data processing change or logging error to an actual traffic problem, wasting investigative effort.
source_title: Debug Google Search Traffic Drops

title: Assuming a spam policy violation or content issue caused an algorithmic traffic drop
description: When a traffic drop is related to an algorithmic update, there might not be anything fundamentally wrong with the content. Incorrectly assuming a violation and making unnecessary content changes can be a strategic mistake.
source_title: Debug Google Search Traffic Drops

title: Attributing traffic drops to algorithmic updates without considering external demand changes
description: Changes in user behaviour due to new trends or seasonality can reduce demand for certain queries independently of rankings. Failing to check Google Trends may lead to incorrectly blaming an algorithm update for a drop that is actually market-wide.
source_title: Debug Google Search Traffic Drops

title: Taking action on small position fluctuations without sufficient cause
description: Search positions are not static or fixed, and a minor drop such as from position 2 to position 4 does not warrant action. Over-reacting to small fluctuations can lead to unnecessary changes that may disrupt what is already working.
source_title: Debug Google Search Traffic Drops

title: Mobile Version of Page Having Different Content or Blocked Resources Compared to Desktop
description: Since Google primarily crawls and indexes the mobile version of pages, having a mobile version with less content, different meta tags, missing structured data, or blocked resources compared to the desktop version will negatively impact indexing and rankings.
source_title: Developer's Guide to Search

title: Pages buried too deeply in the site hierarchy
description: Pages that require an excessive number of clicks to reach from the home page are likely treated as lower priority by Google. A flat, logical hierarchy ensures important pages remain within a reasonable click depth.
source_title: Ecommerce Site Structure – Helping Google Understand Navigation Hierarchy

title: Omitting Required Structured Data on Detail Pages in Summary+Detail Carousel Implementation
description: When using the summary page + multiple detail pages structure for host carousels, each detail page must include the appropriate structured data for its content type (e.g., Recipe, Course, Movie). Failing to add this structured data on detail pages makes the carousel ineligible for rich results.
source_title: Carousel Structured Data – Host Carousels with ItemList

title: Using ItemList Without a Supported Content Type for Host Carousel Eligibility
description: ItemList structured data alone is not sufficient for a host carousel rich result. It must be combined with one of the supported structured data content types (e.g., recipes, courses, movies). Implementing ItemList without the paired supported content type will not qualify the page for a carousel rich result.
source_title: Carousel Structured Data – Host Carousels with ItemList

title: Shutting Down Old Hosting Infrastructure Before Traffic Fully Migrates
description: Decommissioning the old hosting infrastructure before all traffic has moved to the new infrastructure can cause crawl errors, missed requests, and indexing gaps. The old infrastructure should remain active until traffic has fully transitioned.
source_title: Changing Your Web Hosting Without Changing URLs

title: Not Monitoring Server Logs on Both Old and New Infrastructure Post-Migration
description: Failing to watch server logs on both old and new servers after a hosting migration means issues such as residual traffic to old servers, crawl errors, or slow response times on the new infrastructure may go undetected.
source_title: Changing Your Web Hosting Without Changing URLs

title: Leaving Temporary Crawl Blocks Active After Launch
description: Failing to remove temporary blocks (e.g., robots.txt disallow rules or noindex headers) applied during staging/testing before the new infrastructure goes live will prevent Googlebot from crawling and indexing the site, causing ranking drops.
source_title: Changing Your Web Hosting Without Changing URLs

title: Specifying Conflicting Canonical URLs Across Different Methods
description: Using different canonicalization techniques (e.g., rel=canonical, sitemap, redirects) that point to different URLs for the same page sends contradictory signals to Google and undermines canonicalization effectiveness.
source_title: Canonical URLs – How to Specify and Consolidate Duplicate Pages

title: Using the URL Removal Tool for Canonicalization
description: Site owners should not use the URL removal tool to handle duplicate content or indicate canonical preferences. This tool hides all versions of a URL from search entirely, rather than consolidating signals to a preferred canonical.
source_title: Canonical URLs – How to Specify and Consolidate Duplicate Pages

title: Linking to Duplicate URLs Instead of Canonical URLs
description: When linking internally within a site, pointing links to non-canonical (duplicate) URLs dilutes canonicalization signals and may cause Google to favor the wrong version of the page.
source_title: Canonical URLs – How to Specify and Consolidate Duplicate Pages

title: Specifying URL Fragments as Canonical
description: Using URLs with fragment identifiers (e.g., example.com/page#section) as canonical URLs is not supported by Google, which generally ignores URL fragments for canonicalization purposes.
source_title: Canonical URLs – How to Specify and Consolidate Duplicate Pages

title: Using DiscussionForumPosting Markup for Publisher-Authored Content
description: DiscussionForumPosting structured data should only be used to describe user-generated posts. Applying it to content primarily authored by the website's publishers or their agents is a misuse of the schema type and violates the markup guidelines.
source_title: Discussion Forum Structured Data – DiscussionForumPosting

title: Relying Solely on Client-Side Rendering for Important Content
description: Relying on client-side JavaScript rendering instead of server-side rendering or static site generation means content is not present in the initial HTML response. This can introduce indexing delays and risks content not being indexed if Googlebot cannot fully execute the JavaScript.
source_title: Developer's Guide to Search

title: Setting the Canonical via JavaScript Instead of HTML Source
description: For client-side rendered pages, specifying or modifying the canonical URL using JavaScript rather than including it in the static HTML source is unreliable. Google may not execute JavaScript before determining the canonical URL.
source_title: Canonical URLs – How to Specify and Consolidate Duplicate Pages

title: Missing Namespace Declarations for Sitemap Extensions
description: Using sitemap extension tags (e.g., for news, images, or video) without declaring the corresponding namespace in the urlset tag via the xmlns attribute will result in invalid sitemap markup that Google cannot properly parse.
source_title: Combining Sitemap Extensions – Using Multiple Extensions in One File

title: Ignoring a misplaced noindex tag causing page-wide traffic drops
description: A misplaced noindex tag on important pages will cause those pages to be dropped from Google's index, resulting in significant page-wide traffic loss. This is listed as a primary page-wide technical issue to investigate during traffic drop analysis.
source_title: Debug Google Search Traffic Drops

title: Serving Completely Different Content to Crawlers via Dynamic Rendering (Cloaking)
description: Using a dynamic rendering server to deliver content to search engine crawlers that is significantly different from what users see is classified as cloaking, which violates Google's guidelines. Dynamic rendering is only acceptable when the rendered content is similar to what users receive.
source_title: Dynamic Rendering as a Workaround for JavaScript Content

title: Taking action on small positional drops without deeper assessment
description: A small drop in search position, such as from position 2 to position 4, does not warrant action. Reacting to minor positional fluctuations wastes resources and may introduce unnecessary changes, since positions in Google Search results are not static or fixed.
source_title: Debug Google Search Traffic Drops

title: Adding Structured Data to Pages Without Questions
description: Education Q&A structured data must not be added to pages that do not contain questions. Applying this markup to non-question pages violates Google's guidelines and risks manual action.
source_title: Education Q&A Structured Data – Flashcard Pages

title: INP Exceeding 200 Milliseconds Threshold
description: Allowing Interaction to Next Paint to exceed 200 milliseconds signals poor interactivity responsiveness. INP above 500 milliseconds is considered poor and indicates the page is failing to respond adequately to user interactions such as clicks, taps, and keyboard inputs.
source_title: Core Web Vitals – LCP, INP, and CLS Explained

title: Using DiscussionForumPosting Instead of Q&A Markup for Q&A Sites
description: Websites whose primary structure is questions with answers should use Q&A markup, not DiscussionForumPosting. Applying the wrong schema type reduces the accuracy of structured data and may prevent eligibility for appropriate rich result features.
source_title: Discussion Forum Structured Data – DiscussionForumPosting

title: Omitting Full Post Text from DiscussionForumPosting or Comment Elements
description: Each DiscussionForumPosting element must include the entire text of the post, and each Comment element must include the entire text of the response if found on the page. Truncating or omitting content violates the markup requirements and can reduce structured data quality.
source_title: Discussion Forum Structured Data – DiscussionForumPosting

title: Exceeding Sitemap Size Limits in Combined Sitemaps
description: When combining multiple sitemap extensions in a single file, the increased file size may cause the sitemap to exceed the 50 MB uncompressed or 50,000 URL limits. Failing to split oversized combined sitemaps into multiple files violates sitemap requirements and can result in incomplete indexing.
source_title: Combining Sitemap Extensions – Using Multiple Extensions in One File

title: Omitting BreadcrumbList structured data on hierarchical pages
description: BreadcrumbList structured data communicates the hierarchical position of each page within the site to Google. Omitting it means Google must infer hierarchy from other signals alone, reducing its ability to understand the site structure and purpose of different pages.
source_title: Ecommerce Site Structure – Helping Google Understand Navigation Hierarchy

title: Inconsistent URL Case Handling
description: When a web server treats upper and lower case URLs as equivalent but does not consistently normalize them, duplicate content issues arise and crawl efficiency is reduced.
source_title: Ecommerce URL Structure Design Best Practices

title: Internal Linking to Temporary or Session-Based URL Parameters
description: Linking internally to URLs that include temporary parameters such as session IDs, tracking codes, location-relative values, or timestamps wastes crawl budget and creates indexing problems.
source_title: Ecommerce URL Structure Design Best Practices

title: Failing to Handle Empty Category Pages Appropriately
description: Leaving empty category pages (with no items) without proper handling causes low-quality pages to be indexed. They should either receive a noindex robots meta tag or return a 404 status code if the category is removed from navigation.
source_title: Ecommerce URL Structure Design Best Practices

title: Unmanaged faceted navigation generating excessive near-duplicate URLs
description: Faceted navigation creates large numbers of URL combinations through filtering and sorting. Without proper management (e.g., canonicalization, noindex, or robots.txt rules), this leads to excessive crawling of near-duplicate pages, wasting crawl budget and potentially diluting ranking signals.
source_title: Ecommerce Site Structure – Helping Google Understand Navigation Hierarchy

title: Providing Irrelevant Structured Data to Game Enriched Search
description: Marking up content with structured data types that don't accurately reflect the content (e.g., a sports live streaming site labelling broadcasts as local events) violates relevance requirements for enriched search and is explicitly not acceptable.
source_title: Enriched Search Results – Interactive Rich Results with Structured Data

title: Using Review Snippet Structured Data for Employer Ratings
description: Marking up employer ratings with the generic review snippet structured data type instead of the EmployerAggregateRating schema.org type is an outdated practice. Google recommends transitioning to EmployerAggregateRating as the current standard for employer rating markup.
source_title: Employer Aggregate Rating Structured Data – EmployerAggregateRating

title: Rating a Category or List Instead of a Specific Hiring Organisation
description: Applying EmployerAggregateRating structured data to a page that rates a category of employers or a list of companies rather than a single, specific hiring organisation violates Google's content requirements for this markup type.
source_title: Employer Aggregate Rating Structured Data – EmployerAggregateRating

title: Hosting Employer Ratings the Site Does Not Collect from Users
description: Adding EmployerAggregateRating markup when the site does not allow users to post their own ratings, or when the displayed ratings are sourced externally rather than hosted by the site itself, violates the content requirements for this structured data type.
source_title: Employer Aggregate Rating Structured Data – EmployerAggregateRating

title: Omitting the Flashcard Value for eduQuestionType Property
description: All questions on flashcard pages must use the Flashcard value for the eduQuestionType property. Failing to set this value correctly will result in non-compliant structured data that may not be eligible for the education Q&A carousel.
source_title: Education Q&A Structured Data – Flashcard Pages

title: Omitting Best/Worst Rating Values When Using a Non-5-Point Scale
description: Using a rating scale other than the default 1–5 without explicitly specifying the bestRating and worstRating properties causes Google to misinterpret the aggregate score, since it defaults to a 5-point scale.
source_title: Employer Aggregate Rating Structured Data – EmployerAggregateRating

title: Hiding Question Content in Data Files or PDFs Instead of On-Page
description: Questions must be immediately visible to users on the page. Storing questions only in data files, PDFs, or other non-rendered formats violates guidelines and prevents Googlebot from properly crawling and indexing the content.
source_title: Education Q&A Structured Data – Flashcard Pages

title: Using Unrecognizable Location Values in Event Structured Data
description: Providing location information in Event structured data that Google cannot match to a physical location. Location values must be recognizable addresses or place names, otherwise the event may fail validation or be excluded from rich results.
source_title: Event Structured Data – Event Rich Results on Google

title: Omitting Required Event Structured Data Properties
description: Failing to include required properties — name, startDate, and location with address information — in Event structured data. Missing required fields makes the event ineligible to appear in Google's event rich results experience.
source_title: Event Structured Data – Event Rich Results on Google

title: Political Entities Using ClaimReview Structured Data
description: Websites for political entities such as campaigns, parties, or elected officials are explicitly ineligible for ClaimReview structured data. Implementing ClaimReview on such sites violates eligibility requirements and will not result in fact-check rich results in Google Search.
source_title: Fact Check Structured Data – ClaimReview for Fact Checking Articles

title: Using Wrong Location Type for Online Events
description: For online events, failing to set eventAttendanceMode to OnlineEventAttendanceMode and/or not using VirtualLocation for the location property. Using a physical location type for an online-only event is an incorrect implementation that misrepresents the event format.
source_title: Event Structured Data – Event Rich Results on Google

title: Site-Wide Quality Failures Leading to Enriched Search Exclusion
description: If a large portion of a site's content fails to meet the enriched search quality bar, Google's ranking algorithm can exclude the entire site from enriched search results — not just the offending pages.
source_title: Enriched Search Results – Interactive Rich Results with Structured Data

title: Mismatch Between ClaimReview Structured Data and Page Content
description: Including ClaimReview structured data that does not accurately reflect the actual content on the page violates eligibility requirements. Google requires consistency between structured data markup and on-page content; mismatches can result in disqualification from fact-check rich results.
source_title: Fact Check Structured Data – ClaimReview for Fact Checking Articles

title: Not Updating eventStatus for Cancelled, Postponed, or Rescheduled Events
description: Failing to update the eventStatus property when an event is cancelled, postponed, or rescheduled. This leaves inaccurate information in search results, potentially misleading users.
source_title: Event Structured Data – Event Rich Results on Google

title: Failing to Attribute the Claim to a Distinct External Origin
description: The specific claim being assessed must be clearly attributed to a distinct origin separate from the fact-checking site itself. Reviewing claims originated by the same site implementing ClaimReview violates the transparency requirement and undermines eligibility for rich results.
source_title: Fact Check Structured Data – ClaimReview for Fact Checking Articles

title: Using FAQPage Schema on Non-Government/Non-Health Sites
description: FAQPage rich results are exclusively available for well-known, authoritative government-focused or health-focused websites. Implementing FAQPage structured data on general commercial or other types of sites will not produce rich results in Google Search, wasting implementation effort and potentially misleading stakeholders about expected outcomes.
source_title: FAQ Structured Data – FAQPage for Government and Health Sites

title: Using FAQPage Structured Data for Advertising Purposes
description: FAQPage schema must not be used for advertising purposes. Using FAQ structured data to promote products, services, or offers rather than to genuinely inform users with FAQ content violates Google's guidelines and can result in penalties or loss of rich result eligibility.
source_title: FAQ Structured Data – FAQPage for Government and Health Sites

title: Using FAQPage Instead of QAPage for User-Submitted Answers
description: When a page allows users to submit multiple answers to a single question, FAQPage is the wrong schema type. QAPage structured data must be used instead. FAQPage is specifically designed for pages where the site itself provides a single definitive answer per question, not for community-driven or user-generated answer formats.
source_title: FAQ Structured Data – FAQPage for Government and Health Sites

title: Omitting FAQ Answer Content from the Visible Page
description: All FAQ content marked up with FAQPage structured data, including answers hidden behind expandable accordion sections, must be visible to users on the source page. Marking up content that is not present and accessible on the page (e.g., answers that only exist in the structured data but not in the HTML) violates guidelines and constitutes a form of cloaking.
source_title: FAQ Structured Data – FAQPage for Government and Health Sites

title: Using Non-Multiple-of-48px Favicon Dimensions
description: Favicons must be a multiple of 48 pixels square (e.g., 48x48, 96x96, 144x144). Using non-conforming pixel dimensions (e.g., 32x32, 64x64, 128x128) means the favicon may not meet Google's requirements and could be excluded from search results.
source_title: Favicons in Google Search – Guidelines and Requirements

title: Setting Favicon at Subdirectory Level
description: Google Search only supports one favicon per site defined by hostname. Favicons can be set at the domain or subdomain level but not at the subdirectory level. Attempting to define different favicons per subdirectory will not be recognized.
source_title: Favicons in Google Search – Guidelines and Requirements

title: Blocking the Favicon File from Googlebot-Image
description: The favicon file itself must be crawlable by Googlebot-Image. If the favicon URL is blocked via robots.txt or other access restrictions, Google cannot crawl it and will not display it in search results.
source_title: Favicons in Google Search – Guidelines and Requirements

title: Assuming unsupported file types will be indexed by Google
description: Submitting or relying on file formats not in Google's supported list for indexation. Google can only index specific file types; content in unsupported formats will not be crawled or indexed, making it invisible in search results.
source_title: File Types Indexable by Google

title: Using Low max-snippet Setting as a Guaranteed Opt-Out from Featured Snippets
description: Setting a low max-snippet value does not guarantee that Google will stop showing featured snippets for a page. Relying on this method as a definitive opt-out is incorrect; only the nosnippet rule provides a guaranteed exclusion from featured snippets.
source_title: Featured Snippets – How They Work and How to Opt Out

title: Lazy-loading content that requires user interaction to load
description: Implementing lazy-loading that depends on user interactions such as clicks or manual scrolling to trigger content loading. Google Search does not interact with pages by scrolling or clicking, so any content requiring user interaction to appear will be hidden from Google's crawler.
source_title: Fix Lazy Loaded Content for Google Search

title: Using nosnippet When Only Featured Snippets Need to Be Suppressed
description: Applying the nosnippet rule when the goal is only to opt out of featured snippets is overly broad. nosnippet blocks all snippets — including regular search result snippets — which may reduce click-through rates. The correct approach for retaining regular snippets while experimenting with featured snippet suppression is to use max-snippet instead.
source_title: Featured Snippets – How They Work and How to Opt Out

title: Serving files with incorrect or missing Content-Type HTTP headers
description: Google determines file type primarily from the Content-Type HTTP header. If this header is missing or incorrect, Google may misidentify the file type and fail to parse it correctly, leading to incorrect or failed indexing.
source_title: File Types Indexable by Google

title: Showing the Paywall More Than 10 Percent of the Time
description: Displaying a paywall to users more than 10 percent of the time causes significant degradation in user satisfaction. This threshold typically corresponds to approximately 3 percent of the total audience being exposed to the paywall. Exceeding this frequency harms user experience and likely reduces subscription conversion rates.
source_title: Flexible Sampling – Paywalls and Limited Content Access

title: Using relative or dynamic date-based parameters in paginated URLs
description: Using relative elements in paginated URLs such as 'date=yesterday' causes the content shown at a given URL to change over time, making it inconsistent for search engines. Absolute page numbers via query parameters like 'page=12' should be used instead to ensure the same content is always served at a given URL.
source_title: Fix Lazy Loaded Content for Google Search

title: Incorrect hreflang annotations causing wrong canonical selection
description: Misconfigured hreflang annotations can cause Google to select a canonical URL from a different language version than intended, resulting in the wrong page being indexed and served to users.
source_title: Fix Canonicalization Issues

title: Faulty CMS settings generating incorrect or missing canonical tags
description: CMS misconfiguration can automatically produce incorrect canonical tags or omit them entirely, causing Google to select an unintended canonical URL for affected pages.
source_title: Fix Canonicalization Issues

title: Server misconfiguration creating incorrect HTTP headers or redirect chains
description: Server-level issues such as wrong HTTP canonical headers or chained redirects can signal an incorrect canonical to Google, leading to unintended URLs being indexed.
source_title: Fix Canonicalization Issues

title: Syndicated content published without indexing controls or canonical back-links
description: Publishing syndicated content on multiple sites without instructing partners to block indexing or add a canonical pointing to the original causes canonicalization confusion and may result in the syndicated copy being treated as canonical.
source_title: Fix Canonicalization Issues

title: Canonical URL resolving to an unverified Search Console property
description: When Google selects a canonical URL that belongs to a Search Console property not owned or verified by the site owner, traffic data for the duplicate page becomes invisible, preventing monitoring and diagnosis.
source_title: Fix Canonicalization Issues

title: Omitting sequential links in paginated infinite scroll implementations
description: Failing to provide sequential links to individual URLs within a paginated set when using infinite scroll or paginated content loading. Without these links, search engines cannot discover the paginated URLs and their content will not be indexed.
source_title: Fix Lazy Loaded Content for Google Search

title: Relying on Local Storage, Session Storage, or HTTP Cookies for Cross-Page Functionality
description: WRS (Web Rendering Service) clears Local Storage, Session Storage, and HTTP cookies between page loads. Any functionality that depends on data persisting between page loads will not work as expected for Googlebot, potentially causing content to fail to render or behave incorrectly during indexing.
source_title: Fix Search Related JavaScript Problems

title: Single-Page Applications Returning 200 Status Codes for Error Pages
description: Single-page applications that handle errors client-side often return a 200 HTTP status code instead of the appropriate error code (e.g., 404 or 500). This causes Googlebot to treat error pages as valid content and index them, polluting the search index with error pages.
source_title: Fix Search Related JavaScript Problems

title: Using Features That Require User Permission Grants
description: Googlebot will decline user permission requests, so any functionality gated behind browser permissions (e.g., geolocation, notifications, camera access) will not function during rendering. Content or features dependent on these permissions will be unavailable to WRS.
source_title: Fix Search Related JavaScript Problems

title: Using Client-Side Analytics to Monitor Googlebot and WRS Activity
description: Client-side analytics may not provide a full or accurate representation of Googlebot and WRS activity. Relying on tools like Google Analytics to monitor crawl behavior leads to inaccurate conclusions about how Googlebot is interacting with the site.
source_title: Fix Search Related JavaScript Problems

title: Relying Solely on Sitemap Submission for Page Discovery
description: Treating sitemap submission as mandatory or the only mechanism for Google to discover pages is a misconception. Google can discover pages without a sitemap; submission only potentially speeds up the process.
source_title: Get Started with Google Search Console

title: Using Google Trends as the Sole Content Strategy Driver
description: Relying exclusively on Google Trends data to determine what content to create, rather than using it as one input alongside overall business goals and user interests. Trends data should inform strategy, not dictate it entirely.
source_title: Get Started with Google Trends for SEO

title: Skipping Site Ownership Verification Before Accessing Search Console Data
description: Attempting to use Search Console without completing ownership verification will deny access to all available information. Verification is a prerequisite for accessing any Search Console reports or data.
source_title: Get Started with Google Search Console

title: Daily Monitoring of Search Console Without Relying on Email Alerts
description: Logging into Search Console every day is unnecessary and represents inefficient use of resources. Google sends email alerts when new issues are found, and a monthly check cadence (or upon site changes) is the recommended approach.
source_title: Get Started with Google Search Console

title: Omitting Structured Data for Paywalled Content
description: Failing to enclose paywalled content within appropriate structured data prevents Google from differentiating legitimately gated content from cloaking. Without structured data markup, Googlebot may interpret the difference between content served to it versus to users as deceptive cloaking, which can harm search rankings.
source_title: Flexible Sampling – Paywalls and Limited Content Access

title: Not Using Content Fingerprinting for JavaScript and CSS Resources
description: Without content fingerprinting, Googlebot may use cached versions of JavaScript or CSS files even when they have changed, because it can ignore caching headers. This causes Googlebot to render pages using outdated resources, potentially resulting in incorrect or missing structured data.
source_title: Generate Structured Data with JavaScript

title: Duplicating Information in Google Tag Manager Instead of Using Variables
description: Hard-coding structured data values directly in Google Tag Manager instead of using variables to extract data from the page increases the risk of mismatches between page content and the injected structured data, which can cause structured data to be inaccurate or misleading.
source_title: Generate Structured Data with JavaScript

title: Testing JavaScript-Generated Structured Data Using Code Input Instead of URL Input
description: Using the code input option in the Rich Results Test to validate JavaScript-generated structured data is unreliable due to JavaScript limitations such as CORS restrictions. This can produce false positives or missed errors that would not reflect actual Googlebot rendering behavior.
source_title: Generate Structured Data with JavaScript

title: Blocking Googlebot via robots.txt from Important Pages or Resources
description: Inadvertently configuring robots.txt to block Googlebot from accessing important pages or resources prevents crawling and indexing of those pages, causing them to be excluded from Google Search results.
source_title: Get Your Website on Google

title: Providing a Non-Equivalent Mobile Experience Compared to Desktop
description: Since Google primarily uses the mobile version of pages for indexing and ranking, having a mobile experience that differs significantly from or is inferior to the desktop experience leads to poor indexing and ranking outcomes.
source_title: Get Your Website on Google

title: Relying Solely on Googlebot to Discover a New Site Without Proactive Steps
description: While Googlebot eventually discovers most public pages through link-following, relying on passive discovery for new or large sites without submitting a sitemap or verifying Search Console ownership results in delayed and incomplete indexing coverage.
source_title: Get Your Website on Google

title: Requesting a review before fully resolving underlying security vulnerabilities
description: Site owners who request a Safe Browsing review without thoroughly investigating their site, fully removing all malware or harmful content, and addressing root-cause vulnerabilities risk re-entering a cycle of noncompliance. This behavior is explicitly what the Repeat Offenders policy is designed to penalize, and repeated instances result in the 30-day review lockout.
source_title: Google Safe Browsing Repeat Offenders Policy

title: Temporarily cleaning up harmful content to pass a Safe Browsing review, then reverting to harmful behavior
description: Sites that cycle between compliant and noncompliant behavior — removing malware or harmful content only during a review period and then reinstating it afterward — are classified as Repeat Offenders by Google Safe Browsing. This pattern results in a 30-day lockout from requesting further reviews via Search Console and persistent user-facing safety warnings.
source_title: Google Safe Browsing Repeat Offenders Policy

title: Treating Discover Traffic as a Reliable or Primary Traffic Source
description: Discover traffic is explicitly less predictable and less dependable than Search traffic. Treating it as a primary or dependable traffic channel leads to poor planning and misaligned strategy.
source_title: Google Discover – How Content Is Selected and Best Practices

title: Assuming the Reviews System Evaluates Third-Party User Reviews
description: Expecting Google's reviews system to apply to user-generated reviews posted in the reviews section of a product or services page. The system only evaluates first-party standalone review content such as articles, blog posts, and similar pages — not third-party user reviews.
source_title: Google Search Reviews System

title: Publishing Non-Article/Non-Video Content Formats Expecting Discover Recommendations
description: Content formats such as job applications, petitions, forms, code repositories, or satirical content without context are not suitable for Discover's interest-based feed and may not be recommended.
source_title: Google Discover – How Content Is Selected and Best Practices

title: Attempting to Selectively Target Googlebot Desktop vs. Smartphone in robots.txt
description: Both Googlebot Desktop and Googlebot Smartphone obey the same product token in robots.txt, making it impossible to selectively block or allow one crawler type while permitting the other via robots.txt rules.
source_title: Googlebot – What It Is and How It Works

title: Ignoring the 2 MB Crawl Limit by Placing Critical Content Beyond That Threshold
description: Googlebot only crawls the first 2 megabytes of a supported file (64 MB for PDFs). Any content, links, or structured data placed beyond this limit will not be crawled or indexed, making large pages with important content buried deep in the file prone to indexing failures.
source_title: Googlebot – What It Is and How It Works

title: Blocking Googlebot from Fetching Video Files on Explicit Pages
description: Preventing Googlebot from crawling or fetching video files on explicit pages can cause Google to demote or filter those pages. This is especially impactful in Video mode search results.
source_title: Guidelines for Sites with Explicit Content

title: Triggering Age Gates for Googlebot Crawls
description: If an age gate or access restriction is served to Googlebot rather than allowing it to crawl the underlying content, Google cannot index the content properly. Sites should verify Googlebot requests and serve content without triggering the age gate.
source_title: Guidelines for Sites with Explicit Content

title: Blocking Googlebot Based on Locale or Perceived Country
description: Applying different robots.txt rules or robots meta tags depending on which locale version is served violates Google's requirement for consistent crawl rules across all locale variants, potentially causing unintended indexing gaps.
source_title: How Google Crawls Locale Adaptive Pages

title: Blocking Pages from Indexing That Should Be Indexed
description: Pages that are explicitly or accidentally blocked from indexing will not appear in Google's index, regardless of their content quality. Using noindex directives, robots.txt disallow rules, or other blocking mechanisms on pages intended for search visibility prevents them from being served in results.
source_title: How Google Search Works

title: Having Duplicate Pages Without Canonical Signals
description: During indexing, Google identifies duplicate pages and selects a canonical version. Without clear canonical signals, Google may choose the wrong canonical, causing the intended page to be excluded from indexing and losing ranking potential.
source_title: How Google Search Works

title: Neglecting Mobile-Friendliness and Page Speed
description: User experience signals including page speed and mobile-friendliness are explicitly cited as ranking factors. Failing to optimise for these signals reduces a page's ranking potential relative to competitors who do optimise for them.
source_title: How Google Search Works

title: Using Unsupported data-vocabulary.org Markup Format
description: Implementing structured data using the data-vocabulary.org markup format will not qualify pages for Google rich result features. Only JSON-LD, RDFa, and microdata formats are supported, with JSON-LD being recommended.
source_title: How Structured Data Works – Introduction to Rich Results

title: Adding Structured Data About Information Not Visible to Users
description: Including structured data that describes information not actually visible on the page to users violates Google's guidelines, even if the information itself is accurate. Structured data must represent content users can see on the page.
source_title: How Structured Data Works – Introduction to Rich Results

title: Creating Empty Pages Solely to Host Structured Data
description: Creating blank or empty pages with no real content for the purpose of holding structured data is a prohibited practice. Structured data must be placed on pages that actually contain and describe the information being marked up.
source_title: How Structured Data Works – Introduction to Rich Results

title: Providing Incomplete or Inaccurate Recommended Properties to Maximize Coverage
description: Attempting to include every possible recommended structured data property with incomplete or inaccurate data is counterproductive. Fewer but complete and accurate recommended properties are more valuable than exhaustive but unreliable markup.
source_title: How Structured Data Works – Introduction to Rich Results

title: Blocking Structured Data Pages from Googlebot
description: Blocking pages containing structured data via robots.txt disallow rules, noindex directives, or other access control methods prevents Google from reading the structured data, disqualifying those pages from rich result eligibility.
source_title: How Structured Data Works – Introduction to Rich Results

title: Launching a Large Ecommerce Site Without Submitting a Sitemap
description: For sites with a large number of URLs, relying solely on individual URL submission via the URL Inspection tool is insufficient. Failing to submit a sitemap means Google may not discover and index all product and category pages in a timely manner.
source_title: How to Launch an Ecommerce Website on Google

title: Stripping All Image Metadata to Reduce File Size
description: Removing all metadata from images for performance optimization can strip critical rights and licensing information, may be illegal in certain jurisdictions, and prevents Google from surfacing licensing details in Google Images.
source_title: Image License Metadata – Licensable Badge in Google Images

title: Assuming HTTP 2xx Status Guarantees Indexing
description: Believing that returning a 2xx success status code ensures a page will be indexed is incorrect. A 2xx code only means the request was successful; Google may still choose not to index the content for other reasons.
source_title: HTTP Status Codes, Network Errors, and DNS Errors

title: Not Participating in Google Merchant Center for Product Visibility
description: Skipping Google Merchant Center registration prevents the ecommerce site from appearing in experiences such as the Google Shopping tab, limiting product discoverability in search.
source_title: How to Launch an Ecommerce Website on Google

title: Blocking Licensed Images Behind Login or Access Restrictions
description: Pages containing images that require accounts or login to access will not be discoverable by Google, making them ineligible for the Licensable badge in Google Images regardless of how well the metadata is implemented.
source_title: Image License Metadata – Licensable Badge in Google Images

title: Sustained 5xx Server Errors Slowing Googlebot Crawling
description: Persistent 5xx server errors cause Googlebot to reduce its crawl rate for the site, which can delay discovery and indexing of new or updated content. Unresolved server errors that make URLs unreachable also lead to those URLs being removed from the index within days.
source_title: HTTP Status Codes, Network Errors, and DNS Errors

title: Adding Structured Data Metadata Only Once for Reused Images
description: Unlike IPTC metadata which is embedded in the image file and travels with it, structured data must be added for every instance an image is used. Omitting structured data on subsequent pages where the same image appears means those instances lack proper licensing metadata.
source_title: Image License Metadata – Licensable Badge in Google Images

title: Omitting Conversion and Revenue Metrics from Ecommerce Reporting
description: Tracking engagement or traffic metrics alone without including conversion and revenue data produces an incomplete and misleading picture of ecommerce business health. Conversion and revenue always factor into ecommerce business metrics and must be included in any performance analysis.
source_title: Understanding Ecommerce Metrics What to Track and Why It Matters 25

title: Blocking Image URLs in robots.txt While Expecting noindex to Work
description: When using the noindex X-Robots-Tag HTTP response header to remove images, crawling of the image URLs must be allowed. If image URLs are blocked via robots.txt, Googlebot cannot access the HTTP headers and therefore cannot extract the noindex directive, rendering it ineffective.
source_title: Image Removals – Removing Images from Google Search Results

title: Returning 200 Status Code for Non-Existent Pages (Soft 404)
description: Returning a 200 OK status code on a page that tells the user the content does not exist (soft 404) misleads Google into treating the response as valid content rather than a missing page, potentially causing incorrect indexing behavior and wasted crawl budget.
source_title: HTTP Status Codes, Network Errors, and DNS Errors

title: Listing More Than 1,000 Images Per Page URL in a Sitemap
description: Specifying more than 1,000 images for a single page URL in an image sitemap exceeds the supported limit and the excess entries will be ignored by Google.
source_title: Image Sitemaps – Helping Google Find Site Images

title: Using Googlebot-Image Disallow to Remove Images from All Google Search Surfaces
description: Blocking the Googlebot-Image user agent in robots.txt only excludes images from Google Images specifically. It does not remove images from all Google search surfaces including Google Search. To exclude images from all Google searches, the Googlebot user agent must be specified instead.
source_title: Image Removals – Removing Images from Google Search Results

title: Using Deprecated Image Sitemap Tags
description: Including deprecated image sitemap tags such as image:caption, image:geo_location, image:title, and image:license in image sitemaps. Google no longer supports these tags — they have been removed and will be ignored.
source_title: Image Sitemaps – Helping Google Find Site Images

title: Using CSS Background Images for Indexable Content
description: Google does not index CSS background images. Any image intended to appear in search results must be included via standard HTML img elements using the src attribute, not set as CSS backgrounds.
source_title: Image SEO Best Practices for Google Search

title: Omitting the image:loc Tag in Image Sitemap Entries
description: Failing to include the image:loc tag within an image:image element. This is the only required tag in the image sitemap extension; omitting it makes the image entry invalid.
source_title: Image Sitemaps – Helping Google Find Site Images

title: Not Verifying Image Hosting Domains in Search Console
description: Failing to verify image hosting domains (third-party CDNs or external image servers) in Search Console alongside the main domain. Both the domain serving main pages and any image hosting domains should be verified for full image sitemap coverage.
source_title: Image Sitemaps – Helping Google Find Site Images

title: Omitting Fallback src Attribute When Using picture or srcset
description: When using the picture element or the srcset attribute for responsive images, failing to specify a fallback URL via the src attribute of the img element means Google may be unable to find and index the image.
source_title: Image SEO Best Practices for Google Search

title: Using Unsupported Image Formats
description: Google Images only supports BMP, GIF, JPEG, PNG, WebP, and SVG formats. Images in other formats will not be indexed or appear in Google Images results.
source_title: Image SEO Best Practices for Google Search

title: Using Generic or Unrepresentative Images as Primary Page Image
description: When specifying a primary image via schema.org primaryImageOfPage or og:image, using a generic image such as the site logo rather than a relevant, representative image of the page content produces poor signals and undermines search result quality.
source_title: Image SEO Best Practices for Google Search

title: Placing Images Far from Relevant Text or on Irrelevant Pages
description: Google extracts contextual information about images from surrounding page content. Placing images far from relevant text, or hosting them on pages unrelated to the image subject, reduces Google's ability to understand and correctly rank the image.
source_title: Image SEO Best Practices for Google Search

title: Neglecting Low-Position, High-CTR Queries
description: Failing to prioritize queries that show high click-through rates but low average positions is a missed opportunity. These queries signal strong user relevance and improving their ranking position could have a significant impact on overall search performance.
source_title: Improve SEO with a Search Console Bubble Chart in Looker Studio

title: Applying Google Search Central guidance to non-search digital marketing channels
description: The documentation explicitly does not address organic social media, email marketing, or other digital marketing channels outside of Google Search. Applying SEO guidance from this documentation to those channels is a strategic mistake, as the guidance is scoped only to how Google finds and ranks public web content.
source_title: Introduction – Google Search Central Documentation

title: Using Full-Page Interstitials to Obscure Content
description: Covering the entire page with an interstitial overlay (promotional or otherwise) obstructs users and prevents Google from understanding page content, likely leading to poor search performance.
source_title: Interstitials and Dialogs – Avoiding Intrusive Page Overlays

title: Mandatory Interstitials That Completely Block Content from Googlebot
description: Even legally required interstitials (e.g., age gates on casino sites) should overlay content rather than replace it entirely, so Google can still index at least some of the underlying page content.
source_title: Interstitials and Dialogs – Avoiding Intrusive Page Overlays

title: Redirecting Users to a Separate Page for Consent or Input
description: Instead of overlaying an interstitial on the current page, redirecting users to an entirely separate page for consent or input is considered intrusive and should be avoided.
source_title: Interstitials and Dialogs – Avoiding Intrusive Page Overlays

title: Expecting Google Search Central documentation to cover paid advertising (Google Ads)
description: Google Search Central documentation explicitly does not cover paid advertising products such as Google Ads. Practitioners who look to this documentation for guidance on paid search campaigns will find no relevant information, and conflating SEO guidance with paid advertising strategy is a category error.
source_title: Introduction – Google Search Central Documentation

title: Neglecting basic SEO even on automated website platforms
description: Even sites on automated platforms like Blogger, Wix, or Squarespace benefit from learning basic SEO. Ignoring search fundamentals on these platforms can result in missed traffic, as Google may have trouble understanding the pages and the site owner misses out on an important source of relevant users.
source_title: Introduction – Google Search Central Documentation

title: Relying on robots.txt disallow to enforce crawler behavior across all search engines
description: robots.txt rules are not universally supported or enforced. Different crawlers may interpret robots.txt syntax differently, and compliance is voluntary. Assuming all crawlers will respect disallow directives can result in unintended crawling and indexing by non-compliant bots.
source_title: Introduction to robots.txt

title: Using URL Fragments for Client-Side Routing Instead of History API
description: Using URL fragments (hash-based routing) to load different content in single-page applications makes that content uncrawlable by Googlebot. The History API should be used instead for client-side routing to ensure links and content are discoverable.
source_title: JavaScript SEO Basics – How Google Crawls and Renders JavaScript

title: Not Setting jobLocationType to TELECOMMUTE for Remote Job Postings
description: For work-from-home or remote job opportunities, omitting the jobLocationType property set to TELECOMMUTE means the posting will not be correctly identified as remote, causing it to miss remote-job filters and reducing relevance for job seekers specifically searching for remote work.
source_title: Job Posting Structured Data – JobPosting for Job Search Experience

title: Web Component Shadow DOM Content Not Exposed in Rendered HTML
description: Content inside shadow DOM that is not surfaced through slot elements or equivalent mechanisms may not appear in the rendered HTML that Googlebot parses, causing that content to be excluded from indexing.
source_title: JavaScript SEO Basics – How Google Crawls and Renders JavaScript

title: JavaScript Links Not Using Anchor Elements with href Attributes
description: For Googlebot to crawl links in a JavaScript application, they must be standard anchor elements with href attributes. Using onclick handlers, div elements, or other non-anchor mechanisms for navigation prevents Googlebot from discovering and crawling linked URLs.
source_title: JavaScript SEO Basics – How Google Crawls and Renders JavaScript

title: Using Sitemaps Instead of Indexing API for Job Posting URLs
description: Submitting job posting URLs via sitemaps rather than the Indexing API delays Googlebot crawling. Google explicitly recommends the Indexing API for job posting URLs because it prompts Googlebot to crawl pages sooner, meaning relying on sitemaps results in slower indexing and reduced freshness of job listings.
source_title: Job Posting Structured Data – JobPosting for Job Search Experience

title: Leaving Expired Job Postings Active Without Proper Removal Signals
description: Failing to remove or invalidate job postings that are no longer available by not setting validThrough to a past date, not returning a 404/410 status, or not removing the JobPosting structured data leaves stale listings in search results, degrading user experience and potentially harming site credibility.
source_title: Job Posting Structured Data – JobPosting for Job Search Experience

title: Placing JobPosting Structured Data on Non-Leaf Pages
description: Adding JobPosting structured data to listing or category pages rather than the most detailed individual job posting page violates Google's guidance that structured data should be placed on the most detailed leaf page possible, reducing eligibility and accuracy of rich results.
source_title: Job Posting Structured Data – JobPosting for Job Search Experience

title: Defining Multiple Physical Locations as a Single LocalBusiness Entity
description: Businesses with multiple physical locations should define each location as a separate LocalBusiness entity. Combining all locations into one entity prevents Google from accurately representing individual locations in Search and Maps results.
source_title: Local Business Structured Data – LocalBusiness for Knowledge Panels and Maps

title: Relying Solely on JavaScript to Set Canonical URLs Instead of HTML Source
description: Canonical URLs in JavaScript applications should be specified in the HTML source code where possible. Relying on JavaScript to inject canonical tags means they may not be present or processed correctly before rendering, reducing reliability.
source_title: JavaScript SEO Basics – How Google Crawls and Renders JavaScript

title: Using Incorrect HTTP Status Codes to Communicate Page State to Googlebot
description: Meaningful HTTP status codes must be used to communicate page status. Returning a 200 status for pages that are not found or are behind a login causes Googlebot to treat them as valid indexable pages, leading to soft 404s or unintended indexation of restricted content.
source_title: JavaScript SEO Basics – How Google Crawls and Renders JavaScript

title: Using noindex to Reduce Crawl Budget Consumption
description: Site owners sometimes use noindex directives as a strategy to reduce crawl budget consumption. This is ineffective because Googlebot must still crawl the page in order to read and process the noindex tag, meaning the crawl budget is consumed regardless.
source_title: Large Site Owner's Guide to Managing Your Crawl Budget

title: Trying to Increase Crawl Budget by Improving Page Speed of Low-Quality Pages
description: Improving server response times or page speed for low-quality pages will not encourage Google to crawl more of those pages. Crawl demand is driven by content value, not speed alone. Optimizing speed without addressing content quality is an ineffective crawl budget strategy.
source_title: Large Site Owner's Guide to Managing Your Crawl Budget

title: Applying Nofollow Universally to All External Links
description: Adding nofollow to all outbound external links indiscriminately is bad practice. Nofollow should be applied selectively only to untrusted sources. Blanket application ignores the nuance that paid links require 'sponsored' and user-generated links require 'ugc' rel attributes.
source_title: Link Best Practices – Crawlability and Anchor Text

title: Omitting the Required 'name' Property from LocalBusiness Structured Data
description: The 'name' property is required for LocalBusiness structured data. Omitting it means the markup is incomplete and may not be eligible for knowledge panel or Maps rich result features.
source_title: Local Business Structured Data – LocalBusiness for Knowledge Panels and Maps

title: Returning Incorrect Status Codes for Permanently Removed Pages
description: Failing to return a proper 404 or 410 HTTP status code for permanently removed pages wastes crawl budget and can leave ghost URLs in Google's index. Using soft 404s or other non-standard responses instead of the correct status codes is a documented anti-pattern.
source_title: Large Site Owner's Guide to Managing Your Crawl Budget

title: Leaving Important Pages Without Any Internal Links
description: Every page that matters to a site must be reachable via at least one internal link from another page. Pages with no internal links pointing to them are at risk of being missed by Googlebot and unsupported by the site's internal link equity flow.
source_title: Link Best Practices – Crawlability and Anchor Text

title: Using robots.txt Temporarily to Reallocate Crawl Budget
description: Temporarily blocking URLs via robots.txt as a tactic to shift crawl budget toward other parts of a site is explicitly discouraged. This practice does not work as intended for crawl budget management and can cause unintended indexing gaps.
source_title: Large Site Owner's Guide to Managing Your Crawl Budget

title: Providing Both Structured Data and Merchant Center Loyalty Configurations
description: When both MemberProgram structured data markup and Merchant Center loyalty programme configurations are provided simultaneously, Google will ignore the structured data and use the Merchant Center settings instead. Relying on structured data markup when Merchant Center configurations exist leads to the structured data being disregarded.
source_title: Loyalty Program Structured Data – MemberProgram for Membership Benefits

title: Placing MemberProgram Markup Outside the Organisation Type
description: MemberProgram structured data must be nested under the Organisation type on the page where administrative details and policies of the business are specified. Placing it outside this context or on the wrong page violates implementation guidelines and prevents correct processing.
source_title: Loyalty Program Structured Data – MemberProgram for Membership Benefits

title: Failing to Monitor and Disavow Harmful Inbound Links
description: Not periodically reviewing the site's link profile leaves harmful or illegitimately acquired links in place, which can expose the site to negative SEO impacts. Active monitoring and disavowal of such links is required to protect authority.
source_title: Maintaining Your Website's SEO

title: Suppressing Browser or OS Security Dialogs
description: Websites or applications that suppress browser and operating system dialogs that alert users violate Google's unwanted software guidelines and will trigger security warnings.
source_title: Malware and Unwanted Software – What They Are and Google's Guidelines

title: Modifying Browser Settings Outside Chrome Settings Override API
description: Changing a user's default search settings, startup page, or new tab page through any method other than the Chrome Settings Override API is classified as unwanted software behavior and will result in security warnings.
source_title: Malware and Unwanted Software – What They Are and Google's Guidelines

title: Transmitting Private User Data Without Disclosure or Encryption
description: Applications or websites that transmit private user data to servers in ways unrelated to app functionality, without disclosing this to users, or without encryption violate Google's unwanted software guidelines.
source_title: Malware and Unwanted Software – What They Are and Google's Guidelines

title: Intercepting or Degrading SSL/TLS Connections
description: Applications that install root certificate-authority certificates, intercept SSL/TLS connections, or degrade TLS and SSL connection security are flagged as unwanted software by Google.
source_title: Malware and Unwanted Software – What They Are and Google's Guidelines

title: Allowing Crawling of Useless Faceted Navigation URLs
description: Failing to block crawling of faceted navigation URLs that do not need to appear in Google Search wastes crawl budget, slows discovery of new useful URLs, and consumes server resources with no meaningful benefit.
source_title: Managing Crawling of Faceted Navigation URLs

title: Using Non-Standard Characters as URL Parameter Separators in Faceted Navigation
description: Using characters such as commas, semicolons, or brackets as parameter separators in faceted navigation URLs instead of the industry-standard ampersand makes it hard for crawlers to detect and parse parameters correctly.
source_title: Managing Crawling of Faceted Navigation URLs
