title: Adding MathSolver Structured Data to Individual Problem Pages
description: MathSolver structured data must only be added to the home page of the site. Adding it to individual problem pages violates Google's implementation requirements and may result in the structured data being ignored or the site being excluded from the Math Solver search experience.
source_title: Math Solver Structured Data – MathSolver for Educational Tools

title: Inconsistent Filter Order in Path-Encoded Faceted Navigation URLs
description: When filters are encoded in the URL path rather than as query parameters, failing to maintain a consistent logical order of filters creates multiple unique URLs for the same content, multiplying duplicate or near-duplicate pages for crawlers.
source_title: Managing Crawling of Faceted Navigation URLs

title: Returning Non-404 Responses for Empty Filter Combinations
description: When a faceted navigation filter combination returns no results, failing to return an HTTP 404 status code causes crawlers to index or waste budget on empty, valueless pages.
source_title: Managing Crawling of Faceted Navigation URLs

title: Hiding Math Solver Solution Behind Login or Paywall
description: Math solvers that are entirely hidden behind a login or paywall are not eligible for the Math Solver Search feature. Additionally, once a user navigates from Google Search to the site, the solution and step-by-step walkthrough must be immediately accessible without any barriers.
source_title: Math Solver Structured Data – MathSolver for Educational Tools

title: Omitting Canonical URLs on Duplicate Math Solver Pages
description: When multiple copies of the same math solver are hosted under different URLs, each copy must use canonical URLs to help Google efficiently crawl the site. Failing to implement canonicals on duplicate solver pages wastes crawl budget and may cause indexing inefficiencies.
source_title: Math Solver Structured Data – MathSolver for Educational Tools

title: Mobile Site Has Less Content Than Desktop Site
description: When the mobile version of a page contains less primary content than the desktop version, it negatively impacts indexing and ranking because Google uses the mobile version for mobile-first indexing. The indexed content will be incomplete relative to what was intended.
source_title: Mobile First Indexing Best Practices

title: Using rel=next and rel=prev for Pagination
description: Google no longer supports the rel=next and rel=prev link attributes for pagination. Implementing them expecting crawl or indexing benefits for paginated content series will have no effect.
source_title: Meta Tags and HTML Attributes that Google Supports

title: Omitting Explicit bestRating on Non-5-Point Scales
description: Failing to set bestRating explicitly when using a rating scale other than 1–5 causes Google to default to bestRating=5, misinterpreting scores (e.g., a 7/10 rating is read as 7/5, inflating the apparent score incorrectly).
source_title: AggregateRating

title: Using AggregateRating Without Real User Reviews
description: Implementing AggregateRating markup on a page that does not reflect actual user feedback violates Google's structured data policies and can lead to loss of rich result eligibility.
source_title: AggregateRating

title: Setting bestRating Lower Than ratingValue
description: Configuring bestRating to a value lower than ratingValue produces an invalid rating range (ratingValue must fall within worstRating–bestRating), which can cause structured data errors and prevent rich result display.
source_title: AggregateRating

title: Using Merchant Listing Markup on Pages That Link to Other Sellers
description: Merchant listing structured data is only eligible for pages where shoppers can directly purchase a product. Pages that merely link to other sites that sell the product are not eligible for merchant listing experiences and should not use this markup.
source_title: Merchant Listing Structured Data – Products for Sale

title: Applying Merchant Listing Structured Data to Category or Multi-Product Pages
description: Product rich results only support pages focused on a single product or multiple variants of the same product. Category pages listing many different products are not eligible for merchant listing rich results.
source_title: Merchant Listing Structured Data – Products for Sale

title: Providing Both Structured Data and Search Console Return Policy Settings
description: When merchants configure return policies in both structured data markup and Google Search Console's account-level settings, Google will only use the Search Console information and ignore the structured data. This makes structured data implementation effort wasted if Search Console settings also exist.
source_title: Merchant Return Policy Structured Data – MerchantReturnPolicy

title: Spreading Return Policy Information Across Multiple Pages
description: Return policy information should be placed on a single page on the site. Distributing return policy content across multiple pages goes against the recommended implementation and can negatively affect how Google parses and displays this information.
source_title: Merchant Return Policy Structured Data – MerchantReturnPolicy

title: Applying Merchant Listing Markup to Prohibited or Regulated Product Content
description: Content policies prohibit merchant listing markup for content promoting widely prohibited or regulated goods, including firearms and weapons, recreational drugs, tobacco and vaping products, and gambling-related products. Using structured data on such pages violates Google's policies.
source_title: Merchant Listing Structured Data – Products for Sale

title: Using JavaScript-Generated Product Markup Instead of Server-Side HTML
description: Dynamically generating Product structured data via JavaScript makes Google's Shopping crawls less frequent and less reliable. For merchants optimising for all types of shopping results, structured data should be placed in the initial HTML rather than generated dynamically.
source_title: Merchant Listing Structured Data – Products for Sale

title: Defining Member-Specific Shipping Benefits Without a Regular Non-Member Shipping Service
description: When using the validForMemberTier property to designate member shipping benefits in ShippingService structured data, at least one regular non-member shipping service must also be provided. Implementing only member-tier shipping definitions without a standard shipping service violates the schema requirements and may result in incorrect or missing shipping information in search results.
source_title: Merchant Shipping Policy Structured Data – ShippingService

title: Using a Single URL for Products Sold in Multiple Currencies
description: When offering products for sale in multiple currencies, a distinct URL per currency must be used. Serving multi-currency product data under a single URL violates merchant listing requirements.
source_title: Merchant Listing Structured Data – Products for Sale

title: Using OfferShippingDetails at Organisation Level Instead of Product-Specific Offer Level
description: When a specific product requires a different shipping policy than the business standard, OfferShippingDetails should be specified under the Offer structured data type using the shippingDetails property — not nested under Organisation. Misplacing product-specific shipping overrides at the organisation level rather than the offer level leads to incorrect structured data implementation.
source_title: Merchant Shipping Policy Structured Data – ShippingService

title: Hosting Open Comment Forms or User-Generated Content Without Spam Prevention
description: Leaving comment forms and user-generated content inputs unprotected allows spammers to add spammy content to your site, which can violate Google's spam policies and negatively impact search performance.
source_title: Preventing and Monitoring Abuse on Your Site – Overview

title: Failing to Add HTTPS Property Separately in Search Console During HTTPS Migration
description: When migrating from HTTP to HTTPS, treating the HTTPS version as the same Search Console property as the HTTP version is an error. Google Search Console treats HTTP and HTTPS as separate properties, each requiring independent verification and monitoring.
source_title: Move a Site with URL Changes – Migrations and Domain Changes

title: Prioritizing HTTPS Over High-Quality Content as a Ranking Strategy
description: Treating HTTPS migration as a significant ranking lever rather than focusing on high-quality content is a strategic mistake. The HTTPS ranking signal provides only a small boost and is explicitly less important than content quality.
source_title: Move a Site with URL Changes – Migrations and Domain Changes

title: Not Mapping Every Old URL to a Corresponding New URL During Migration
description: Failing to create individual redirect mappings for every old URL to its specific new URL during a site migration results in broken redirects, lost link equity, and poor user experience. Every old URL must have a corresponding permanent redirect to its new equivalent.
source_title: Move a Site with URL Changes – Migrations and Domain Changes

title: Using JavaScript to Inject or Modify Meta Tags
description: Injecting or changing meta tags via JavaScript is discouraged because crawlers may not reliably execute JavaScript, leading to meta tag instructions being missed or ignored by Google's crawler.
source_title: Meta Tags and HTML Attributes that Google Supports

title: Relying on the Keywords Meta Tag for Ranking
description: The keywords meta tag is not used by Google for ranking. Including it provides no SEO benefit and wastes implementation effort that could be directed toward supported signals.
source_title: Meta Tags and HTML Attributes that Google Supports

title: Using Dynamic or Randomized Image URLs on Mobile Pages
description: When image URLs change each time a page loads, Google cannot reliably crawl or cache those images, which disrupts image indexing and can cause image-related ranking signals to be lost.
source_title: Mobile First Indexing Best Practices

title: Lazy-Loading Primary Content Behind User Interactions
description: Requiring user interactions such as swiping, clicking, or typing to reveal primary content prevents Google from loading and indexing that content during mobile-first crawling, leading to incomplete indexing.
source_title: Mobile First Indexing Best Practices

title: Assuming Movie Carousel Rich Results Appear on Desktop
description: The movie carousel structured data feature is only available on mobile devices. Implementing it with the expectation that it will generate rich results on desktop search will lead to incorrect assumptions about visibility and performance.
source_title: Movie Carousel Structured Data – Movie Rich Results on Mobile

title: Using Non-Crawlable Resources on Mobile Site
description: If resources such as images, scripts, or stylesheets are blocked from crawling on the mobile site, Google cannot fully render and evaluate the page, which can harm indexing quality and ranking.
source_title: Mobile First Indexing Best Practices

title: Omitting or Mismatching Image Alt Text on Mobile Versus Desktop
description: Using different or missing alt text on mobile images compared to desktop images results in inconsistent signals to Google during mobile-first indexing, reducing accessibility compliance and potentially weakening image search relevance.
source_title: Mobile First Indexing Best Practices

title: Omitting Required ListItem Properties in Movie Carousel Markup
description: Each ListItem in the itemListElement array requires both a position property (indicating order starting from 1) and a url property pointing to the individual movie detail page. Omitting either property makes the carousel markup non-compliant and may prevent rich result eligibility.
source_title: Movie Carousel Structured Data – Movie Rich Results on Mobile

title: Including News Articles Older Than Two Days in the News Sitemap
description: News sitemaps should only contain URLs for articles published within the last two days. Keeping older article URLs in the news sitemap with news:news metadata violates Google's news sitemap guidelines and is unnecessary.
source_title: News Sitemaps – Submitting News Articles to Google

title: Hosting Malware or Unwanted Software Without Awareness
description: Site owners who host downloadable files without verifying their safety risk unknowingly distributing content classified as malware, which triggers Google security warnings and decreases search traffic.
source_title: Preventing and Monitoring Abuse on Your Site – Overview

title: Using Organisation Schema for a Person Entity
description: Applying Organisation structured data to represent an individual person instead of using the Person schema.org type leads to incorrect entity classification and can harm disambiguation efforts in search results.
source_title: Organisation Structured Data – Helping Google Understand Your Business Entity

title: Omitting Required Tags in the News Sitemap news:news Element
description: Each news:news element must include the required tags: news:publication (with child elements news:name and news:language), news:publication_date, and news:title. Missing any of these required elements results in an invalid news sitemap.
source_title: News Sitemaps – Submitting News Articles to Google

title: Returning Personal or Geographical Data About Recipients/Senders in Package Tracking API
description: Package Tracking APIs must not return personal data or geographical information about the recipient or sender of a package. Including such data in API responses violates participation requirements and could disqualify a company from the Early Adopters Programme.
source_title: Package Tracking – Early Adopters Program for Delivery Companies

title: Using Generic Organisation Type When a More Specific Subtype Applies
description: Using the base Organisation schema type when a more specific subtype such as Corporation, LocalBusiness, GovernmentOrganisation, or EducationalOrganisation is applicable reduces semantic precision and misses opportunities for better entity classification.
source_title: Organisation Structured Data – Helping Google Understand Your Business Entity

title: Omitting Entity Disambiguation Properties on Organisation Schema
description: Failing to include disambiguation properties such as iso6523, naics, and sameAs leaves Google unable to reliably differentiate the organisation from others with similar names, weakening entity presence in knowledge panels and search results.
source_title: Organisation Structured Data – Helping Google Understand Your Business Entity

title: Exceeding API Response Time Thresholds for Package Tracking
description: The package tracking API must respond within 700 milliseconds on average, with the 95th percentile not exceeding 1000 milliseconds. APIs that fail to meet these latency requirements do not satisfy the technical eligibility criteria for participation in the programme.
source_title: Package Tracking – Early Adopters Program for Delivery Companies

title: Relying on the noindex tag to block content from all search engines
description: The noindex tag only affects Google and search engines that explicitly support it. Assuming that adding a noindex tag will prevent content from appearing on all search engines or prevent direct user access is incorrect. Other search engines may still index and display the page.
source_title: Page Removals – How to Remove a Page from Google Search

title: Applying for Package Tracking Programme Without Meeting Geographic Eligibility
description: Only delivery companies based in India, Japan, or Brazil — or those that are the sole authorised provider of tracking information for companies servicing those areas — are eligible for the Early Adopters Programme. Companies outside these criteria will not qualify regardless of technical readiness.
source_title: Package Tracking – Early Adopters Program for Delivery Companies

title: Using Infinite Scroll Without a Paginated URL Fallback
description: Infinite scroll implementations that do not provide distinct URLs for each content chunk prevent Google crawlers from accessing all content, since crawlers cannot simulate user scrolling behavior. A paginated fallback with distinct URLs is required for full crawlability.
source_title: Pagination and Incremental Page Loading for Ecommerce

title: Relying on JavaScript Buttons or User-Triggered Actions to Load Paginated Content
description: Google's crawlers do not click buttons and generally do not trigger JavaScript functions that require user actions to update page contents. Using a 'Load More' button or similar interaction without providing crawlable anchor links with href attributes means Google cannot discover all paginated content.
source_title: Pagination and Incremental Page Loading for Ecommerce

title: Treating Removals tool requests as permanent page suppression
description: Removal requests made via the Removals tool in Search Console expire after approximately 6 months, meaning the page can reappear in search results. Relying solely on this tool for long-term or permanent removal without implementing a more lasting method is a strategic mistake.
source_title: Page Removals – How to Remove a Page from Google Search

title: Using robots.txt to block pages from appearing in search results
description: Blocking a URL via robots.txt does not prevent it from being indexed and appearing in Google Search results. A URL can still be indexed even if crawling is blocked, making robots.txt an ineffective and unreliable method for removal from search results.
source_title: Page Removals – How to Remove a Page from Google Search

title: Using Subdirectory URLs to Promote Preferred Sources Eligibility
description: Subdirectory-level URLs are not eligible to appear in the Google source preferences tool. Only domain-level and subdomain-level sites qualify. Promoting or linking to a subdirectory URL (e.g., https://www.example.com/blog) in the context of Preferred Sources will not work, as it is ineligible for the feature.
source_title: Preferred Sources – Helping Your Audience Follow Your Publication

title: Using rel=next and rel=prev Expecting Google to Recognize Pagination Relationships
description: Google no longer uses rel=next and rel=prev link attributes to identify pagination relationships. Relying on these attributes for Google SEO purposes has no effect and may lead to misplaced confidence in pagination signal implementation.
source_title: Pagination and Incremental Page Loading for Ecommerce

title: Using Relative Parameters Instead of Absolute Page Numbers in Paginated URLs
description: Using relative parameters (e.g., offset-based or session-relative values) rather than absolute page numbers in paginated URLs can result in inconsistent content being served at the same URL across different loads, violating the requirement that each URL display the same content every time it is loaded.
source_title: Pagination and Incremental Page Loading for Ecommerce

title: Allowing Sorting and Filtering Variations to Be Indexed Without Duplication Controls
description: Failing to handle sorting and filtering URL variations carefully results in Google indexing many near-duplicate versions of the same content list, diluting crawl budget and potentially causing duplicate content issues.
source_title: Pagination and Incremental Page Loading for Ecommerce

title: Publishing Web Stories Without Valid AMP or Required Metadata
description: Web Stories must be valid AMP and include all required metadata fields (publisher-logo-src, poster-portrait-src, title, publisher). Missing any of these disqualifies the story from appearing in Google Search Web Stories experiences.
source_title: Web Stories – Enable, Best Practices, and Content Policy

title: Web Stories That Require External Links to Access Essential Information
description: Web Stories must be complete, self-contained experiences. Requiring users to leave the story to access essential information via external links violates content policy.
source_title: Web Stories – Enable, Best Practices, and Content Policy

title: Omitting priceCurrency in AggregateOffer markup
description: priceCurrency is a mandatory field in AggregateOffer. Leaving it out means the currency of the aggregated price range is undefined, violating schema requirements and likely disqualifying the markup from rich results.
source_title: AggregateOffer

title: Using Web Stories Primarily as Advertisements
description: Web Stories whose sole purpose is to advertise a product or service, especially where the creator directly benefits from user consumption, are not allowed and risk permanent exclusion from rich Web Stories experiences.
source_title: Web Stories – Enable, Best Practices, and Content Policy

title: Providing an image smaller than 1200px wide in BlogPosting schema
description: Google recommends images of at least 1200px wide for article rich results. Providing an ImageObject with a width below this threshold may prevent the image from being used in rich results displays.
source_title: BlogPosting

title: Failing to Monitor Search Console Security Issues Report
description: Site owners who do not regularly monitor the Security Issues report in Google Search Console may miss Google's detection of malware, phishing, or other security issues on their site, delaying response and prolonging harm.
source_title: Prevent a Malware Infection – Security Best Practices for Site Owners

title: Failing to noindex New User Profile Pages by Default
description: New user profile pages should have a noindex tag applied by default to prevent spammy or low-quality user-generated profile content from being indexed by Google. Omitting this allows spammers to instantly get their content indexed.
source_title: Prevent User Generated Spam on Your Site

title: Not Applying ugc or nofollow to User-Submitted Links
description: Links posted by untrusted users should automatically have the ugc or nofollow rel attribute applied. Failing to do so passes PageRank through potentially spammy links and may violate Google's link spam guidelines.
source_title: Prevent User Generated Spam on Your Site

title: Allowing Instant Publishing of User Content Without Moderation
description: Allowing spammers to instantly publish user-generated content without manual approval or moderation enables spam to proliferate rapidly. Implementing moderation or manual approval significantly reduces spam.
source_title: Prevent User Generated Spam on Your Site

title: Not Using CAPTCHA or Verification on Sign-Up and Submission Forms
description: Sign-up and content submission forms without reCAPTCHA or similar verification tools allow automated bots to submit spam content at scale, overwhelming moderation efforts and polluting the site with low-quality content.
source_title: Prevent User Generated Spam on Your Site

title: Failing to Monitor and Address Security Issues After Detection
description: Not using the Security Issues report in Search Console to identify, investigate, and resolve detected threats — and not requesting a review after fixes — leaves Google warnings active, significantly reducing search traffic.
source_title: Preventing and Monitoring Abuse on Your Site – Overview

title: Using Unvetted Third-Party Resources Such as Ads and Images
description: Embedding third-party resources like ads and images without vetting them can introduce social engineering attacks or phishing content into a site, triggering security warnings and harming user trust and search visibility.
source_title: Preventing and Monitoring Abuse on Your Site – Overview

title: Adding Pros and Cons Structured Data to Non-Editorial Pages
description: Pros and cons structured data (positiveNotes/negativeNotes) is only eligible for editorial product review pages. Applying it to merchant product pages or customer product reviews will not qualify for this feature and the markup will be ignored.
source_title: Product Snippet Structured Data – Non Merchant Product Pages

title: Using Product Snippet Markup on Merchant/Purchase Pages
description: Product snippet structured data is designed for non-merchant pages (editorial reviews, comparisons, aggregators) where users cannot directly purchase. Pages where customers can buy products directly should use merchant listing markup instead. Using product snippet markup on purchasable pages is incorrect implementation.
source_title: Product Snippet Structured Data – Non Merchant Product Pages

title: Missing unique identifiers or URLs for individual product variants
description: Each individual product variant must have unique identifiers and unique URLs. Failing to provide distinct identifiers and URLs for each variant means the structured data will not be processed correctly by Google, making products ineligible for variant display in merchant listing experiences.
source_title: Product Variants Structured Data – ProductGroup for Size and Color Variants

title: Using AggregateOffer to Describe Product Variants
description: AggregateOffer is intended to represent an aggregation of offers from multiple merchants, not to describe a set of product variants. Using it for product variants is an incorrect application of this schema type.
source_title: Product Snippet Structured Data – Non Merchant Product Pages

title: Marking Up Cars with Only the Car Type When Attaching Ratings
description: When using Product structured data for cars and attaching ratings, both the Car type and the Product type must be included together. Using only the Car type is insufficient because Car is not automatically recognized as a subtype of Product for this purpose, which will result in ratings not being properly supported.
source_title: Product Snippet Structured Data – Non Merchant Product Pages

title: Specifying variant-common properties on each individual Product instead of at the ProductGroup level
description: Properties that apply to all variants, such as brand and review information, should be specified once at the ProductGroup level. Duplicating or misplacing these properties on individual Product variants rather than the group is an inefficient and incorrect use of the ProductGroup schema design.
source_title: Product Variants Structured Data – ProductGroup for Size and Color Variants

title: Using QAPage Markup for Pages Without User-Submitted Answers
description: QAPage markup should only be used when users are able to submit answers to a single question on the page. If the site provides a single definitive answer without user-submitted alternatives, using QAPage markup is incorrect and FAQPage markup should be used instead.
source_title: Q&A Page Structured Data – QAPage for Questions and Answers

title: Missing ItemList Structured Data for Recipe Carousel Eligibility
description: To appear in a recipe carousel or grid, both ItemList structured data summarising the recipes AND individual recipe structured data must be provided, along with a summary page listing all recipes. Omitting ItemList or the summary page disqualifies the content from carousel/grid rich results.
source_title: Recipe Structured Data – Rich Results for Recipes

title: Using FAQPage Markup for Pages With User-Submitted Answers
description: FAQPage markup is intended for content where a single definitive answer is provided by the site. Using FAQPage on pages where multiple users can submit answers misrepresents the content type and the correct markup would be QAPage.
source_title: Q&A Page Structured Data – QAPage for Questions and Answers

title: Using Recipe Structured Data for Non-Recipe Content
description: Applying Recipe schema markup to content that is not about preparing a particular dish (e.g., facial scrub instructions, party planning ideas) is invalid and may prevent the content from appearing as a rich result.
source_title: Recipe Structured Data – Rich Results for Recipes

title: Omitting Required QAPage Structured Data Properties
description: QAPage markup requires specific properties to be eligible for rich results. Missing the mainEntity property, Question name, or acceptedAnswer with a text property means the markup is incomplete and the page will not qualify for rich result display.
source_title: Q&A Page Structured Data – QAPage for Questions and Answers

title: Neglecting Structured Data Implementation for Rich Results
description: Failing to provide structured data markup on pages means Google cannot enable rich result features for that content. Without structured data, pages are ineligible for enhanced search appearances such as recipe cards, review snippets, job postings, event listings, and other rich result types that can significantly improve click-through rates and search visibility.
source_title: Ranking and Search Appearance – Overview

title: Using JavaScript-Based or Meta Refresh Redirects Instead of Server-Side Redirects
description: Relying on JavaScript-based redirects or meta refresh redirects instead of server-side HTTP 301/308 redirects reduces the reliability with which Google correctly interprets the redirect, potentially harming canonicalization and indexing of the target URL.
source_title: Redirects and Google Search – Types, Implementation, and Canonicalization

title: Adding unnecessary rel attributes to normal editorial links
description: Normal links that Google should follow unconditionally require no rel attribute. Adding nofollow or other qualifying attributes to standard editorial links unnecessarily restricts Google from following them, potentially undermining the site's own PageRank flow and link equity.
source_title: rel Attributes – Qualifying Outbound Links for Google

title: Not using rel=ugc for user-generated content links
description: Links inserted by users in comments, forum posts, or other user-generated content areas should be marked with rel=ugc. Failing to do so misrepresents the editorial nature of those links to Google.
source_title: rel Attributes – Qualifying Outbound Links for Google

title: Using Wrong Structured Data Type for Employer Reviews
description: Sites that provide reviews about employers should use EmployerAggregateRating structured data rather than generic AggregateRating or Review types, which are not the appropriate format for this use case.
source_title: Review Snippet Structured Data – Stars and Ratings in Search Results

title: Self-Serving Reviews: Business Controlling Its Own Review Markup
description: If the entity being reviewed controls the reviews about itself, pages using LocalBusiness or any Organisation type of structured data are not eligible for the star review feature. This applies whether reviews are placed directly in structured data or through an embedded third-party widget.
source_title: Review Snippet Structured Data – Stars and Ratings in Search Results

title: Scraped Content Republished Without Transformation or Added Value
description: Copying content from other websites verbatim or aggregating content from multiple sources without editorial curation, transformation, or meaningful added value is prohibited as scraped content spam.
source_title: Spam Policies – Machine Generated Content Through Scam and Fraud

title: Assuming Meeting Search Essentials Guarantees Indexing or Ranking
description: Site owners sometimes believe that if a page meets Google's Search Essentials (technical requirements, spam policies, best practices), it is guaranteed to be indexed and ranked. In reality, meeting the essentials only makes a page eligible for consideration — many additional factors influence whether and how a page appears in results.
source_title: Search Essentials – Overview

title: Omitting Required Review Properties in Structured Data
description: Review structured data without the required 'author' (reviewer's name) and 'reviewRating' properties will be invalid and ineligible for rich result features including star snippets.
source_title: Review Snippet Structured Data – Stars and Ratings in Search Results

title: Ignoring Search Essentials for Non-English or Non-US Content
description: Some site owners assume Google's Search Essentials apply only to English-language or US-hosted content and may not apply their SEO practices universally. The Search Essentials apply to all content regardless of language, hosting country, or content type.
source_title: Search Essentials – Overview

title: Engaging in Practices That Violate Spam Policies
description: Using techniques that attempt to manipulate ranking signals or deceive users constitutes a violation of Google's Spam Policies, which are part of the Search Essentials. Such violations can result in complete removal of pages or an entire site from Google Search results.
source_title: Search Essentials – Overview

title: Dynamically Adding or Removing data-nosnippet via JavaScript
description: Google explicitly states that data-nosnippet should not be added to or removed from existing nodes through JavaScript. Doing so may result in unpredictable or ignored snippet suppression behavior.
source_title: Robots Meta Tag, data nosnippet, and X Robots Tag Specifications

title: Requiring Login or Returning HTTP Errors for Crawlable Pages
description: Pages that require a login to access or return HTTP error codes when accessed by Googlebot are not crawlable and will not be indexed. Ensuring Googlebot receives a successful HTTP response is a baseline technical requirement.
source_title: Search Essentials – Technical Requirements

title: Serving Different Content to Googlebot Than to Users (Cloaking)
description: Showing different content to Googlebot than to regular users is called cloaking and is a Google spam policy violation. It is treated as deceptive manipulation regardless of intent and can result in removal from the index.
source_title: Search Essentials – Technical Requirements

title: Blocking Pages with robots.txt While Expecting Them to Be Indexed
description: Blocking a page via robots.txt prevents Googlebot from crawling it, meaning any noindex tag on the page cannot be seen. However, the page may still appear in search results if discovered through links. Relying on robots.txt to remove pages from the index is ineffective and unreliable.
source_title: Search Essentials – Technical Requirements

title: Blocking CSS, JavaScript, or Images via robots.txt
description: Blocking page resources such as CSS, JavaScript, or images through robots.txt prevents Googlebot from fully rendering the page, which can cause content to not be extracted correctly and harm indexing.
source_title: Search Essentials – Technical Requirements

title: Using Browser Features Not Supported by Googlebot's Rendering Engine
description: Pages that depend on specific browser features unsupported by Googlebot's rendering engine risk having their content incorrectly extracted or not extracted at all, preventing proper indexing.
source_title: Search Essentials – Technical Requirements

title: Relying Solely on Structured Data Without Google Merchant Center for Shopping Surfaces
description: Using only structured data on product pages without uploading a product feed to Google Merchant Center limits eligibility for key Google surfaces. Participation in Google Merchant Center is mandatory for some surfaces, such as the Google Shopping tab, and some experiences combine data from both sources. Relying on structured data alone means missing out on richer appearances and reduced coverage.
source_title: Share Product Data with Google – Structured Data and Merchant Center

title: Omitting Pricing Data from Both Structured Data and Merchant Center Feed
description: If pricing data is absent from structured data on the page, Google may fall back to the Merchant Center feed to populate product snippets. However, if neither source contains pricing data, product snippets may be incomplete or ineligible for rich results, reducing search appearance quality.
source_title: Share Product Data with Google – Structured Data and Merchant Center

title: Skipping SXG Validation Before Deployment
description: Deploying signed exchanges without verifying they meet Google's cache requirements can result in SXG indexing errors and loss of prefetching benefits. The SXG Validator Chrome extension should be used to confirm cache compliance before relying on SXG for performance gains.
source_title: Signed Exchanges – Prefetching for Better Page Experience

title: Using robots.txt carelessly to block important pages
description: The robots.txt file can prevent crawling of pages, but using it without care can inadvertently block pages that should be indexed. Careless use of robots.txt risks hiding important content from Google.
source_title: SEO Starter Guide

title: Serving Invalid or Unparseable Signed Exchanges Without Fallback
description: If Googlebot cannot parse an SXG, it recrawls the URL without the SXG Accept header to retrieve the plain HTML variant. If SXG indexing errors occur, Google links to the original URL without SXG. Failing to maintain a valid HTML fallback or ignoring SXG validation errors can result in crawl inefficiency and loss of prefetching benefits.
source_title: Signed Exchanges – Prefetching for Better Page Experience

title: Pursuing link-building tactics that violate Google's spam policies
description: Attempting to build authority through link schemes or other tactics that violate Google's spam policies is explicitly prohibited and unsustainable. Google rewards genuine links earned through quality content, not manipulative link-building.
source_title: SEO Starter Guide

title: Defining Site Names at Subdirectory Level
description: Google Search does not support site names defined at the subdirectory level. Only domain or subdomain level site names are recognized. Attempting to set a site name via structured data on a subdirectory page will be ignored.
source_title: Site Names in Google Search

title: Using Generic or Non-Unique Site Names
description: Using generic names as the preferred site name is unlikely to be selected by Google unless the brand is extremely well-recognised. The site name should be unique, accurately reflect the site's identity, and not be misleading.
source_title: Site Names in Google Search

title: Omitting Structured Data from Duplicate Home Pages
description: When a site has duplicate home pages (e.g., HTTP and HTTPS versions), failing to place the same WebSite structured data on all duplicates can cause inconsistencies in how Google reads the preferred site name.
source_title: Site Names in Google Search

title: Creating Multiple WebSite Structured Data Blocks for Site Name
description: If WebSite structured data already exists on the site, adding a separate WebSite structured data block solely for site name properties is incorrect. The site name properties must be nested within the existing WebSite structured data block.
source_title: Site Names in Google Search

title: Using Unclear or Non-Compact Page Titles and Headings
description: Page titles and headings that are uninformative, irrelevant, or overly verbose reduce the quality of sitelinks. Google uses title and heading text to generate sitelinks, so poor text choices directly impair sitelink generation.
source_title: Sitelinks – How Google Generates Navigation Links for Sites

title: Unclear or Illogical Site Structure and Internal Linking
description: Sites lacking a clear, logical hierarchy with meaningful internal links prevent Google's algorithms from identifying useful sitelinks. Without a well-structured navigation that indicates page relationships and hierarchy, sitelinks may not be generated.
source_title: Sitelinks – How Google Generates Navigation Links for Sites

title: Hosting Referenced Sitemaps on a Different Site Without Cross-Site Setup
description: Sitemaps referenced in a sitemap index file must be hosted on the same site as the sitemap index file. Referencing sitemaps from a different domain without configuring cross-site submission through Search Console violates this requirement and will cause indexing issues.
source_title: Sitemap Index Files – Managing Multiple Sitemaps

title: Exceeding the 50,000 URL or 50 MB Limit Per Sitemap Without Splitting
description: A single sitemap file must not exceed 50,000 URLs or 50 megabytes uncompressed. Failing to split oversized sitemaps and use a sitemap index file will result in the sitemap being invalid and URLs potentially not being discovered.
source_title: Sitemap Index Files – Managing Multiple Sitemaps

title: Placing Referenced Sitemaps Higher in the Directory Hierarchy Than the Sitemap Index File
description: Sitemaps referenced in a sitemap index file must be in the same directory as the sitemap index file or lower in the site hierarchy. Placing individual sitemaps in a parent directory above the sitemap index file violates the protocol.
source_title: Sitemap Index Files – Managing Multiple Sitemaps

title: Using Cross-Site Sitemaps Without All Sites Verified in Search Console or Listed in robots.txt
description: Cross-site sitemaps only work if all referenced sites have been verified in Search Console or if the sitemaps are listed in the robots.txt files of their individual hosts. Failing to meet either condition means cross-site sitemaps will not function correctly.
source_title: Sitemap Index Files – Managing Multiple Sitemaps

title: Assuming All Sitemap Items Will Be Crawled and Indexed
description: Site owners sometimes treat a sitemap submission as a guarantee that all listed URLs will be crawled and indexed by Google. A sitemap only helps Google discover URLs more efficiently — it provides no guarantee of crawling or indexing for any item listed.
source_title: Sitemaps – What They Are and When to Use One

title: Creating Sitemaps for Small, Well-Linked Sites Without Media or News Content
description: Investing resources in building and maintaining sitemaps for sites with fewer than roughly 500 indexable pages that are already comprehensively interlinked and lack rich media or news content provides little to no benefit, representing wasted effort.
source_title: Sitemaps – What They Are and When to Use One

title: Using keyword strings instead of descriptive meta descriptions
description: Meta descriptions that consist of long strings of keywords rather than genuine, human-readable summaries fail to inform users and reduce the quality of search traffic. Google explicitly recommends truly descriptive content over keyword stuffing in this field.
source_title: Snippets and Meta Descriptions

title: Marking Up Apps That Cannot Be Downloaded or Used
description: App structured data should only be applied to software applications that users can actually download or use. Marking up pages for apps that are unavailable, discontinued, or not accessible to users violates the requirement that structured data must reflect real, accessible content.
source_title: Software App Structured Data – SoftwareApplication Rich Results

title: Structured Data Not Reflecting Visible Page Content
description: SoftwareApplication structured data must match what is actually visible and accessible to users on the page. Providing markup with details such as price, ratings, or operating system that differ from or are absent from the visible page content constitutes a mismatch violation.
source_title: Software App Structured Data – SoftwareApplication Rich Results

title: Omitting Required Properties from SoftwareApplication Markup
description: The SoftwareApplication schema requires name, offers (with price), and at least one of aggregateRating or review. Omitting any of these required properties will result in invalid structured data that cannot generate rich results.
source_title: Software App Structured Data – SoftwareApplication Rich Results

title: Hosting Social Engineering or Phishing Content
description: Sites that trick users into revealing confidential information (passwords, phone numbers, SSNs) or downloading dangerous software are flagged as deceptive. This includes content that impersonates trusted entities like banks, browsers, or government institutions.
source_title: Social Engineering – Phishing and Deceptive Sites

title: Building Links Designed to Manipulate PageRank Rather Than Provide User Value
description: Creating links intended to manipulate Google's quality assessment — including paid links passing PageRank, reciprocal link exchanges, automated link-building services, forum/blog comment links placed for SEO rather than user value, and private blog networks — constitutes link spam and is prohibited.
source_title: Spam Policies – Cloaking Through Link Spam

title: Insufficiently Labelled Third-Party Services Operating on Behalf of Another Entity
description: When a third party operates a site or service on behalf of another entity without making the relationship clear to users, Google can flag this as social engineering, triggering deceptive site warnings.
source_title: Social Engineering – Phishing and Deceptive Sites

title: Cloaking: Showing Different Content to Crawlers vs. Users
description: Presenting different content, pages, or redirects to search engine crawlers than to human visitors is prohibited regardless of the reason. This includes using JavaScript, CSS, or redirect techniques to deceive Google about a page's true content.
source_title: Spam Policies – Cloaking Through Link Spam

title: Creating Doorway Pages Targeting Search Engines Instead of Users
description: Building pages or sites designed primarily for search engines — such as multiple near-duplicate pages targeting specific regions, cities, or keywords — that funnel users to other pages rather than providing standalone value is a prohibited spam practice.
source_title: Spam Policies – Cloaking Through Link Spam

title: Hidden Text and Links to Manipulate Search Rankings
description: Placing text or links on a page in ways visible to search engines but not to users — such as using tiny font sizes, matching text color to background, placing content behind images, or using CSS to move content off-screen — is a prohibited manipulation tactic.
source_title: Spam Policies – Cloaking Through Link Spam

title: Keyword Stuffing to Manipulate Rankings
description: Loading a page with excessive or repetitive keywords — including repeated phrases, blocks of city/state names, or lists of phone numbers without substantive content — in an attempt to artificially inflate relevance signals is prohibited.
source_title: Spam Policies – Cloaking Through Link Spam

title: Missing Self-Referencing hreflang Link on Page Variants
description: Each page variant must include a complete set of hreflang link elements that includes a reference to itself. Omitting the self-referencing link from any variant creates an incomplete and potentially invalid annotation set.
source_title: Tell Google About Localized Versions of Your Pages – hreflang Annotations

title: Expecting Quick Recovery After Spam Policy Compliance
description: Webmasters often expect rankings to recover within days or weeks after bringing a site into compliance with spam policies. However, Google's systems require months to reassess compliance, meaning short-term expectations of recovery are unrealistic and can lead to premature conclusions that remediation efforts failed.
source_title: Spam Updates – What They Are and How to Respond

title: Assuming Link Spam Cleanup Will Restore Previous Rankings
description: After a link spam update, any ranking benefits previously generated by spammy links are permanently removed. Cleaning up link spam will not regain those lost ranking benefits, meaning sites cannot expect to return to pre-update ranking levels solely by removing or disavowing spammy links.
source_title: Spam Updates – What They Are and How to Respond

title: Violating Spam Policies Without Anticipating Ranking Suppression
description: Sites that violate Google's spam policies risk ranking lower in results or being removed from results entirely. Failing to proactively review and comply with spam policies leaves sites vulnerable to significant visibility loss when spam updates are applied.
source_title: Spam Updates – What They Are and How to Respond

title: Sneaky Redirects That Send Users to a Different Page Than What Google Indexed
description: Showing Google a page optimised for a specific query and then redirecting actual users to a page with unrelated or different content is a form of cloaking and is explicitly prohibited. This manipulates Google's understanding of the page while deceiving users.
source_title: Spam Policies – Machine Generated Content Through Scam and Fraud

title: Marking Confusing Audio Content with Speakable Structured Data
description: Content that sounds confusing in voice-only situations — such as datelines, photo captions, and source attributions — should not be marked with speakable structured data. Marking such elements degrades the audio experience and misuses the markup's intent.
source_title: Speakable Structured Data – Audio Playback for News Content

title: Marking an Entire Article Instead of Key Points with Speakable Markup
description: Speakable markup should focus on key points rather than highlighting an entire article. The optimal speakable content per section is approximately 20–30 seconds (two to three sentences). Over-marking dilutes the effectiveness of the markup and goes against Google's guidance.
source_title: Speakable Structured Data – Audio Playback for News Content

title: Deploying Structured Data on Pages Under Manual Action
description: A manual action applied against a page causes its structured data to be completely ignored by Google. Implementing or relying on structured data for rich result eligibility on pages with active manual actions is ineffective and will not produce rich results.
source_title: Structured Data Deployment Workflow – How to Build, Test, and Release

title: Omitting ItemList Structured Data in Combination with Supported Content Types
description: The structured data carousels beta requires ItemList markup to be used in combination with at least one supported structured data content type. Using only a supported content type without ItemList, or using ItemList alone, makes the page ineligible for the beta carousel rich result.
source_title: Structured Data Carousels Beta – New Host Carousel Format

title: Applying Established Host Carousel Structured Data Rules to the Beta Carousel
description: Unlike the established host carousel, the beta carousel only requires structured data to be added to the summary page, not to individual detail pages. Applying the established host carousel's markup requirements (e.g., adding structured data to all detail pages) to the beta format is unnecessary and indicates a misunderstanding of the beta feature's distinct requirements.
source_title: Structured Data Carousels Beta – New Host Carousel Format

title: Omitting VideoObject structured data on product pages containing videos
description: When product pages include video content but VideoObject structured data is not implemented, Google lacks the machine-readable signals needed to understand and index the video, reducing its visibility in search.
source_title: Structured Data for Ecommerce Sites – Relevant Schema Types

title: Using ProductGroup schema for single-variant products
description: ProductGroup structured data is specifically designed for products that exist in multiple variants. Using ProductGroup for a product with only a single variant is an incorrect implementation that misrepresents the product structure to Google.
source_title: Structured Data for Ecommerce Sites – Relevant Schema Types

title: Using AI or Automated Tools to Generate Content Primarily for Ranking Manipulation
description: Generating large volumes of content using AI or automated tools without genuine intent to help users — including spinning, paraphrasing, or translating existing content and presenting it as original — is prohibited. AI-generated content is not inherently banned, but content created primarily to manipulate search rankings rather than genuinely serve users violates Google's spam policies.
source_title: Spam Policies – Machine Generated Content Through Scam and Fraud

title: Skipping Validation with the Rich Results Test Before Deployment
description: Deploying structured data without first validating it using the Rich Results Test risks publishing markup with critical errors that disqualify pages from rich result eligibility. Errors identified by the test must be fixed prior to deployment.
source_title: Structured Data Deployment Workflow – How to Build, Test, and Release

title: Failing to Use Structured Data for Indexed Paywalled Content
description: Sites that offer subscription-based or registration-gated content intended to be crawled and indexed must implement schema.org structured data with isAccessibleForFree and hasPart/WebPageElement markup. Omitting this structured data causes Google to potentially treat the content as cloaking, which is a spam policy violation.
source_title: Subscription and Paywalled Content Structured Data

title: Assuming Correct Structured Data Guarantees Rich Result Appearance
description: Even when structured data is implemented correctly and passes all validation tests, Google does not guarantee it will appear as a rich result in search. Treating correct implementation as sufficient for rich result eligibility leads to false expectations and misattributed performance issues.
source_title: Structured Data Deployment Workflow – How to Build, Test, and Release

title: Inconsistent Bot Access Policy Between AMP and Non-AMP Pages
description: The authorisation endpoint must grant Googlebot access consistently across both AMP and non-AMP versions of paywalled pages. Having different access policies between AMP and non-AMP pages creates inconsistency that can undermine crawling and indexing integrity.
source_title: Subscription and Paywalled Content Structured Data

title: Nesting Paywalled Content Sections
description: Content sections marked as paywalled using the hasPart/WebPageElement pattern must not be nested within each other. Nesting paywalled sections can cause implementation errors and is explicitly disallowed in the structured data specification.
source_title: Subscription and Paywalled Content Structured Data

title: Using Non-Class Selectors in cssSelector Property
description: The cssSelector property on WebPageElement instances must use only class selectors to identify paywalled sections. Using ID selectors, element selectors, attribute selectors, or other CSS selector types is not supported and can result in incorrect or failed paywall markup implementation.
source_title: Subscription and Paywalled Content Structured Data

title: Consistently low-quality structured data causing site-wide exclusion from enriched search
description: If Google's enriched search ranking algorithm determines that a large portion of a site's structured data does not meet the quality bar, the entire site can be excluded from enriched search results, not just individual pages.
source_title: Structured Data General Guidelines

title: Providing only required structured data properties while ignoring recommended ones
description: Completeness of structured data directly impacts eligibility and quality scoring for enriched search results. Providing only minimum required properties without recommended ones reduces the quality rating of the item and its chances of appearing in rich results.
source_title: Structured Data General Guidelines

title: Using structured data that violates Google's spam or content policies
description: Structured data that violates Google's content or spam policies can result in a manual action, removing the page's eligibility for rich result appearances. This is a compliance violation with direct enforcement consequences.
source_title: Structured Data General Guidelines

title: Using Invalid Region Codes in hreflang Annotations
description: Using non-standard region codes such as EU, UN, or UK in hreflang attributes causes the annotations to be ignored by Google Search. Region codes must follow ISO 3166-1 Alpha 2 format (e.g., GB instead of UK).
source_title: Tell Google About Localized Versions of Your Pages – hreflang Annotations

title: Omitting the x-default hreflang Fallback for Non-Matched Language Users
description: Not including an x-default hreflang value means users whose browser language settings do not match any localized version of the site have no designated fallback page, reducing the effectiveness of hreflang implementation.
source_title: Tell Google About Localized Versions of Your Pages – hreflang Annotations

title: Taking the Entire Site Offline Instead of Limiting Functionality
description: Completely removing a site from Google's index by taking it fully offline is a significant change that can be very difficult to recover from. There is no fixed recovery time and no mechanism to speed it up. Businesses experiencing temporary disruptions should keep the site online and limit functionality instead.
source_title: Temporarily Pause or Disable a Website

title: Omitting Title Elements on Pages
description: Pages that lack a title specified in the title HTML element miss the primary signal Google uses for generating title links, forcing Google to rely on less reliable secondary sources.
source_title: Title Links in Google Search – How They Are Generated and Best Practices

title: Using 503 for Extended Downtime Beyond One to Two Days
description: Returning a 503 HTTP status code is appropriate only for urgent, very short outages of one to two days at most. For longer required downtime, an indexable placeholder home page with a 200 HTTP status code should be used instead. Applying 503 for extended periods risks compounding index loss.
source_title: Temporarily Pause or Disable a Website

title: Including Flight Price Information in Title Elements
description: Including rapidly changing data such as flight prices in title elements is explicitly discouraged by Google, as prices change too frequently to remain accurate in cached or indexed title links.
source_title: Title Links in Google Search – How They Are Generated and Best Practices

title: Using Vague or Non-Descriptive Title Text
description: Using vague descriptors like 'Home' for the home page or 'Profile' for a specific person's profile page in title elements reduces the quality of title links and fails to give users a quick insight into the content of the page.
source_title: Title Links in Google Search – How They Are Generated and Best Practices

title: Using Boilerplate Titles Across Many Pages
description: Using the same or very similar titles across many pages reduces the quality of title links, as Google rewards unique, page-specific titles that clearly differentiate content.
source_title: Title Links in Google Search – How They Are Generated and Best Practices

title: Using Unnecessarily Long or Verbose Title Text
description: Unnecessarily long or verbose title text degrades the quality of title links. Titles should be descriptive and concise rather than padded with excessive wording.
source_title: Title Links in Google Search – How They Are Generated and Best Practices

title: Top Places List Eligibility: Sponsored or Non-Independent Lists
description: A top places list that is sponsored, not genuinely curated, or lacks editorial independence will not be eligible to appear as a Top Places List rich result. Only lists that are genuine, independent, and not sponsored qualify for this feature.
source_title: Top Places List – Site Eligibility for Business Mentions

title: Top Places List Eligibility: Automated or Templated List Content
description: Lists consisting of templated sentences built from data or automated metrics are not eligible for the Top Places List rich result. Google requires genuine curation rather than algorithmically or template-generated list entries.
source_title: Top Places List – Site Eligibility for Business Mentions

title: Attempting to Directly Request Top Places List Rich Results
description: Site owners cannot directly request or guarantee inclusion of their lists in Top Places List rich results. Attempting to do so or expecting a guaranteed outcome misunderstands how eligibility works — Google's systems determine it automatically.
source_title: Top Places List – Site Eligibility for Business Mentions

title: Assuming Top Places List Feature Applies to Businesses Without a Physical Location
description: The Top Places List rich result feature is only available for businesses with a physical location. Assuming that online-only or virtual businesses can benefit from this feature is incorrect and will produce no results.
source_title: Top Places List – Site Eligibility for Business Mentions

title: Expecting Same-Day Crawling for New URLs
description: Assuming that newly published URLs will be crawled on the same day is incorrect. New pages typically take several days at minimum to be noticed and crawled by Googlebot, and planning or reporting timelines should account for this delay.
source_title: Troubleshoot Google Search Crawling Errors

title: Using robots.txt as a Temporary Crawl Budget Reallocation Mechanism
description: Rotating sitemaps or dynamically adding and removing pages from robots.txt to manage crawl budget is explicitly not recommended. robots.txt should reflect stable, long-term crawling intent, not be used as a short-term lever for budget management.
source_title: Troubleshoot Google Search Crawling Errors

title: Relying on Search Console for Per-URL Crawl History
description: Search Console does not provide crawl history filtered by individual URL or path. Assuming it does and using it as the sole tool for per-URL crawl investigation leads to incomplete diagnostics. Server logs must be used instead to verify whether specific URLs have been crawled by Googlebot.
source_title: Troubleshoot Google Search Crawling Errors

title: Assuming Good Site-Wide Signals Guarantee High Rankings for All Pages
description: Believing that strong site-wide signals (e.g., domain authority, overall site quality) will automatically cause all pages on a site to rank highly is a flawed assumption. Google's ranking systems operate at the page level, meaning individual pages are evaluated on their own merits alongside site-wide signals.
source_title: Understanding Google Search Ranking Systems

title: Focusing on Only One or Two Page Experience Aspects
description: Site owners who focus narrowly on one or two aspects of page experience (e.g., only Core Web Vitals or only HTTPS) miss the holistic evaluation Google's ranking systems apply. Google rewards an overall great page experience across many aspects, not optimization of isolated signals.
source_title: Understanding Page Experience in Google Search

title: Assuming Good Core Web Vitals Scores Guarantee Top Rankings
description: Treating Core Web Vitals scores as sufficient for top search rankings is a strategic mistake. Google explicitly states that good Core Web Vitals results do not guarantee top rankings, as ranking depends on many additional factors beyond page experience signals.
source_title: Understanding Page Experience in Google Search

title: Using Excessive Ads or Intrusive Interstitials That Interfere with Main Content
description: Pages that use excessive advertising or intrusive interstitials that distract from or interfere with main content fail a key page experience assessment criterion, which can negatively affect how Google's ranking systems evaluate the page.
source_title: Understanding Page Experience in Google Search

title: Applying Page Experience Optimizations at the Site Level Instead of Per Page
description: Assuming that fixing page experience issues on some pages covers the entire site is incorrect. Core Web Vitals and page experience signals are evaluated on a per-page basis, meaning each individual page must meet good experience standards.
source_title: Understanding Page Experience in Google Search

title: Using Non-Standard Elements Inside the HTML <head>
description: The HTML standard only permits specific elements inside <head>: title, meta, link, script, style, base, noscript, and template. Using any other element (e.g., div, img, p) renders the head invalid, causing Google to ignore all subsequent elements and potentially losing critical metadata signals.
source_title: Use Valid HTML to Specify Page Metadata

title: Placing Invalid HTML Elements Before Critical Metadata in <head>
description: When invalid HTML elements (such as div, p, or img) appear in the <head> section before important metadata elements, Google ignores all elements that follow the first invalid element. This means canonical tags, robots meta tags, meta descriptions, and structured data placed after an invalid element will not be processed.
source_title: Use Valid HTML to Specify Page Metadata

title: Expecting Search Console and Google Analytics Data to Match Exactly
description: Search Console and Google Analytics use different metrics and measurement systems, so treating their data as directly comparable or expecting identical numbers leads to incorrect analysis and faulty SEO decisions.
source_title: Using Search Console and Google Analytics Together for SEO

title: Analysing Per-Query Metrics Without Blending Search Console and Analytics Data
description: Attempting to derive per-query performance insights by looking at Search Console or Google Analytics in isolation, rather than blending or joining the datasets using shared dimensions like country, device, and landing page, limits the depth of analysis achievable.
source_title: Using Search Console and Google Analytics Together for SEO

title: Omitting the Required 'name' Property from VacationRental Markup
description: The 'name' property is required for VacationRental structured data. Omitting it means the markup is incomplete and the listing may not be eligible for rich result enhancements in Google Search.
source_title: Vacation Rental Structured Data – VacationRental Listings

title: Structured Data That Does Not Reflect Visible Page Content
description: VacationRental structured data must accurately reflect the content visible on the page. Marking up information in structured data that is not present or visible to users on the page violates Google's guidelines and may result in a manual action.
source_title: Vacation Rental Structured Data – VacationRental Listings

title: Blocking Vacation Rental Pages from Crawling or Indexing
description: Vacation rental pages blocked by robots.txt, noindex tags, or login requirements are not eligible for rich results. Even if structured data is correctly implemented, Google cannot surface enhanced listings for pages it cannot access or index.
source_title: Vacation Rental Structured Data – VacationRental Listings

title: Using URL Fragments to Load Video Content
description: Fragment identifiers (e.g., #timestamp or #video) should not be used to load videos, as Google Search generally does not support URL fragments. Videos loaded via fragment-based URLs will not be discoverable or indexable by Google.
source_title: Video SEO Best Practices for Google Search

title: Blocking Googlebot from the Video Streaming File URL
description: If Googlebot is blocked from fetching the actual video streaming file URL (e.g., via robots.txt), Google cannot index the video content, making it ineligible for video features in search results.
source_title: Video SEO Best Practices for Google Search

title: Blocking the Video Watch Page with robots.txt or noindex
description: If the page hosting the video is blocked by robots.txt or marked with a noindex robots meta tag, Google will not index the page and the video will not appear in search results or be eligible for video features.
source_title: Video SEO Best Practices for Google Search

title: Using Deprecated Video Sitemap Tags
description: Using deprecated tags such as video:category, video:gallery_loc, the autoplay and allow_embed attributes of video:player_loc, video:price, and video:tvshow in video sitemaps. Google has deprecated these and they will be ignored or unsupported.
source_title: Video Sitemaps and Alternatives

title: Incorrect Duration Format in VideoObject
description: Specifying the video duration in a format other than ISO 8601 is incorrect and may prevent the duration from being parsed and displayed in rich results.
source_title: Video Structured Data – VideoObject, Clip, and BroadcastEvent

title: Not Creating Dedicated Watch Pages for Each Video
description: To be eligible for video features such as video results on the main search results page, Video mode, Key Moments, and the Live Badge, each video should have a dedicated watch page whose primary purpose is to present that single video. Embedding multiple videos on a general-purpose page reduces eligibility for these features.
source_title: Video SEO Best Practices for Google Search

title: Article Images Below Minimum Width Requirements
description: Using images narrower than 1200px wide for Article structured data (or 696px for AMP) will prevent eligibility for rich results. Google enforces minimum image dimensions for Article markup.
source_title: ImageObject

title: Using an Unstable or Changing Video File URL
description: The video file must be available at a stable URL. Frequently changing or rotating video file URLs prevents Google from reliably crawling and indexing the video, reducing its visibility in search.
source_title: Video SEO Best Practices for Google Search

title: Using Streaming Protocols for Video Files in Sitemaps
description: Referencing video files via streaming protocols (e.g., RTMP, HLS-only) in video sitemaps is not supported by Google. Files must be accessible via HTTP or FTP.
source_title: Video Sitemaps and Alternatives

title: Listing Videos Unrelated to Host Page Content in Video Sitemap
description: Including videos in a video sitemap that are unrelated to the content of the host page they are listed under violates Google's requirements for video sitemaps and can undermine the sitemap's effectiveness.
source_title: Video Sitemaps and Alternatives

title: Blocking Video Sitemap Files from Googlebot
description: Files referenced in a video sitemap must be fully accessible to Googlebot. Blocking them via robots.txt, firewalls, login requirements, or metafiles prevents Google from crawling and indexing the video content.
source_title: Video Sitemaps and Alternatives

title: Omitting the url Property from ImageObject
description: The url property is the only MUST-have field for ImageObject. Omitting it produces invalid structured data that cannot be processed for rich results or image entity recognition.
source_title: ImageObject

title: Missing Required VideoObject Properties
description: Omitting any of the required VideoObject properties (name, description, thumbnailUrl, uploadDate) will prevent video content from qualifying for video rich results in Google Search.
source_title: Video Structured Data – VideoObject, Clip, and BroadcastEvent

title: Using Incorrect Duration Format in VideoObject
description: Specifying video duration in a format other than ISO 8601 is an implementation error that can cause the structured data to be invalid or ignored by Google.
source_title: Video Structured Data – VideoObject, Clip, and BroadcastEvent

title: Ignoring mobile display differences when optimising for search result visual elements
description: Search result visual elements can be displayed differently depending on whether the user is on a desktop or phone. Optimising only for desktop appearance without considering mobile rendering risks a poor or inconsistent experience across devices, which can affect how results are perceived and interacted with.
source_title: Visual Elements Gallery – Google Search Result Building Blocks

title: Leaving Demo or Staging Site Accessible to Crawlers
description: Accidentally leaving a demo or staging version of a site accessible to search engine crawlers creates duplicate content variants. Google may index these pages and dilute canonicalization signals, causing the wrong version to be selected as canonical or wasting crawl budget.
source_title: What is URL Canonicalization

title: Requesting SafeSearch Review Too Early After Making Changes
description: Site owners should not request a SafeSearch review immediately after making corrections. The classifiers that process and categorize content take up to two to three months to re-process changes. Submitting a review before this period elapses is premature and unlikely to result in a successful outcome.
source_title: What to Do If Your Site Is Incorrectly Flagged as Explicit

title: Assuming Blurred Explicit Images Prevent SafeSearch Flagging
description: Blurring explicit images on a page does not exempt the page from being classified as explicit. If the blurred images can be unblurred by the user, or if the page leads to an unblurred version of the content, the page will still be deemed explicit by SafeSearch systems.
source_title: What to Do If Your Site Is Incorrectly Flagged as Explicit

title: Expecting Google to Overturn Automatic SafeSearch Classifications Without Clear Evidence
description: Google only overturns automatic SafeSearch decisions in cases where a site has clearly been incorrectly categorized. Expecting a review to succeed for nuanced or borderline cases — such as sites with significant nudity or suggestive content — is an unrealistic assumption given how SafeSearch operates.
source_title: What to Do If Your Site Is Incorrectly Flagged as Explicit

title: Skipping Google Merchant Center for Shopping Tab Visibility
description: Failing to participate in Google Merchant Center means ecommerce products will not appear on the Google Shopping tab, which is a dedicated high-intent surface for product discovery.
source_title: Where Ecommerce Content Can Appear on Google

title: Submitting IndexNow Pings for Cosmetic or Non-Substantive Changes
description: Using IndexNow to notify search engines of cosmetic changes such as reformatting, design updates, or metadata-only changes wastes crawl budget and dilutes the signal value of IndexNow submissions. The protocol should only be triggered when meaningful content changes occur — new data, changed recommendations, updated steps, or corrected facts.
source_title: Bing IndexNow Real Time Indexing for AI Visibility

title: Waiting for Standard Crawl Cycles Instead of Submitting IndexNow Immediately on Publication
description: For time-sensitive content where freshness determines citation eligibility in AI answer engines, relying on standard crawl discovery rather than immediate IndexNow submission can result in delayed or missed indexing. Because Bing's index feeds Microsoft Copilot and ChatGPT's Browse mode, delays of days in crawl cycles directly reduce the window in which updated content can be cited.
source_title: Bing IndexNow Real Time Indexing for AI Visibility

title: Assuming IndexNow Accelerates Google Indexing
description: IndexNow does not affect Google. Google does not participate in the IndexNow protocol and uses its own crawl scheduling independently. Publishers who rely solely on IndexNow submissions expecting Google indexing acceleration will experience no benefit for Google-driven visibility. For Google-specific fast indexing, the URL Inspection tool's 'Request Indexing' feature in Google Search Console must be used separately.
source_title: Bing IndexNow Real Time Indexing for AI Visibility

title: Excessive Repeated Pings for the Same URL Without Genuine Content Changes
description: Submitting IndexNow notifications for the same URL multiple times without genuine content changes wastes crawl budget allocated to the domain. This can reduce the effectiveness of legitimate submissions and signal noise to participating engines.
source_title: Bing IndexNow Real Time Indexing for AI Visibility

title: Applying NOARCHIVE Meta Tag to Content Intended for Copilot Citation
description: Using the NOARCHIVE robots meta directive on pages that should appear in Copilot responses prevents those pages from being used in Copilot grounding results and citations entirely, removing them from AI answer eligibility.
source_title: Bing Webmaster Guidelines AI and Copilot Grounding

title: Applying NOCACHE Meta Tag to Pages Intended for High-Quality Copilot Citation
description: Using the NOCACHE robots meta directive severely limits Copilot to only the URL, title, and snippet of the page, preventing it from accessing full content for grounding, which dramatically reduces citation quality and usefulness.
source_title: Bing Webmaster Guidelines AI and Copilot Grounding

title: Hiding Key Content Behind Interactive Elements
description: Placing essential content inside accordions, tabs, carousels, or other interactive elements that require user clicks or scrolling to reveal means Bingbot will not index that content, making it ineligible for both traditional ranking and Copilot grounding.
source_title: Bing Webmaster Guidelines AI and Copilot Grounding

title: Generating Content at Scale Without Editorial Oversight
description: Publishing large volumes of AI-generated or automatically produced content without quality control, accuracy review, or editorial oversight risks exclusion from Bing's index entirely, eliminating any possibility of citation in Copilot or traditional search.
source_title: Bing Webmaster Guidelines AI and Copilot Grounding

title: Placing Key Facts or Critical Information in Images Instead of HTML Text
description: Content embedded in JPEGs, PNGs, SVGs, infographics, rendered charts, or PDFs is invisible to Bing's crawler and therefore cannot be used by Copilot or ChatGPT for AI grounding. Any information that exists only in image or PDF format will be excluded from AI-generated answers regardless of its importance.
source_title: Bing Copilot Content Structure for AI Grounding

title: Covering Multiple Distinct Intents on a Single URL
description: Pages that address multiple unrelated user intents have a lower probability of being selected for any specific Copilot query. Single-intent pages are more likely to match a discrete query and be cited as grounding content.
source_title: Bing Copilot Content Structure for AI Grounding

title: Using Vague Promotional Language That Cannot Be Grounded in AI Responses
description: Unverifiable superlatives and promotional phrases such as 'industry-leading', 'best-in-class', and 'cutting edge' are not factual claims and will not be selected for Copilot grounding. AI systems require verifiable, specific facts and will bypass promotional language when constructing answers.
source_title: Bing Copilot Content Structure for AI Grounding

title: Burying Key Answers After Lengthy Preamble Within a Content Block
description: Placing the core answer or fact at the end of a content block after extensive context-setting reduces the probability of Copilot extracting and citing that block. AI systems favour blocks where the answer or key fact appears at the very start.
source_title: Bing Copilot Content Structure for AI Grounding

title: Assuming Declining Click Metrics Indicate Reduced AI Visibility
